questions.question: Find the mean of 2, 4, 6.
questions.explanation: The sum is 2+4+6=12, and 12 divided by 3 values equals 4.
questions.theory: The mean (arithmetic average) is found by adding all the values together and dividing by how many values there are. It summarizes a data set with a single central number.

questions.question: Find the median of 3, 7, 7, 10, 12.
questions.explanation: With five ordered values, the middle (third) value is 7.
questions.theory: The median is the middle value of a data set when the numbers are arranged in order. With an odd count of values, it is the single value in the center.

questions.question: Find the mode of 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 6, 9.
questions.explanation: The value 6 appears three times, more often than any other value.
questions.theory: The mode is the value that occurs most frequently in a data set. A data set can have one mode, more than one mode, or no mode at all.

questions.question: Find the range of 5, 8, 2, 10, 6.
questions.explanation: The range is the largest value (10) minus the smallest value (2), which equals 8.
questions.theory: The range measures spread by subtracting the smallest value from the largest value in a data set. A larger range indicates the data is more spread out.

questions.question: True or False: The mean of a data set is always one of the values in the data set.
questions.explanation: The mean can be a number that does not appear in the data, such as the mean of 2 and 5 being 3.5.
questions.theory: The mean is a calculated average and need not equal any actual data value. In contrast, the mode and median often (but not always) are actual values from the set.

questions.question: Find the mean of 10, 20, 30, 40.
questions.explanation: The sum is 100, and 100 divided by 4 values equals 25.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the number of values. It is the most common measure of central tendency.

questions.question: Find the median of 4, 8, 6, 2, 10.
questions.explanation: Ordered, the values are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and the middle value is 6.
questions.theory: To find the median you must first sort the data in order, then locate the central value. The data must always be ordered before reading off the middle.

questions.question: Find the mode of 1, 2, 2, 3, 4.
questions.explanation: The value 2 appears twice while every other value appears once.
questions.theory: The mode is the most frequently occurring value. If only one value repeats, that value is the mode.

questions.question: Find the range of 12, 15, 9, 20.
questions.explanation: The range is 20 minus 9, which equals 11.
questions.theory: The range is the difference between the maximum and minimum values. It is the simplest measure of how spread out the data is.

questions.question: Find the median of 1, 3, 5, 7 (an even number of values).
questions.explanation: With four values, the median is the average of the two middle values, (3+5)/2 = 4.
questions.theory: When a data set has an even number of values, the median is the mean of the two central values. This may produce a value not in the data set.

questions.question: Find the mean of 5, 5, 5, 5.
questions.explanation: All four values are 5, so their average is also 5.
questions.theory: When every value in a data set is identical, the mean equals that common value. The mean always lies between the smallest and largest values.

questions.question: Find the mode of 7, 8, 9, 10.
questions.explanation: No value repeats, so the data set has no mode.
questions.theory: If no value occurs more than once, a data set has no mode. The mode only exists when at least one value repeats more than the others.

questions.question: True or False: The range can be a negative number.
questions.explanation: The range is the maximum minus the minimum, so it is always zero or positive.
questions.theory: Because the largest value is never less than the smallest value, the range is never negative. A range of zero means all values are the same.

questions.question: Find the mean of 3, 6, 9, 12, 15.
questions.explanation: The sum is 45, and 45 divided by 5 values equals 9.
questions.theory: The mean is the total of the values divided by the count of values. For evenly spaced numbers, the mean equals the middle value.

questions.question: Find the median of 2, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.
questions.explanation: With six ordered values, the median is the average of the third and fourth values, (4+6)/2 = 5.
questions.theory: For an even number of values, the median averages the two middle values. The values must be in order first.

questions.question: Find the mode of 3, 3, 3, 5, 5, 7.
questions.explanation: The value 3 appears three times, more than any other value.
questions.theory: The mode is the value with the highest frequency. When counts differ, the value appearing most often is the single mode.

questions.question: Find the range of 100, 50, 75, 60.
questions.explanation: The range is 100 minus 50, which equals 50.
questions.theory: The range is computed as the maximum value minus the minimum value. Only the two extreme values affect it.

questions.question: Find the mean of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
questions.explanation: The sum is 21, and 21 divided by 6 values equals 3.5.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum divided by the number of values, and it can be a decimal. It need not be a whole number even when all data are whole numbers.

questions.question: Find the median of 9, 1, 5.
questions.explanation: Ordered, the values are 1, 5, 9, and the middle value is 5.
questions.theory: The median of three values is the middle one after sorting. Always arrange the numbers in order before identifying the center.

questions.question: Find the mode of 6, 6, 7, 7, 8.
questions.explanation: Both 6 and 7 appear twice, so the set has two modes: 6 and 7.
questions.theory: A data set with two values tied for the most occurrences is called bimodal and has two modes. The mode is not always a single value.

questions.question: Find the range of 7, 7, 7, 7.
questions.explanation: The maximum and minimum are both 7, so the range is 7 minus 7, which equals 0.
questions.theory: When all values are identical, the maximum equals the minimum and the range is zero. A zero range indicates no spread in the data.

questions.question: True or False: A data set can have more than one mode.
questions.explanation: If two or more values tie for the most occurrences, the set has multiple modes.
questions.theory: Unlike the mean and median, which are unique, the mode can be a single value, several values, or none. Sets with two modes are called bimodal.

questions.question: Find the mean of 0, 5, 10.
questions.explanation: The sum is 15, and 15 divided by 3 values equals 5.
questions.theory: Zero is a valid data value and is included when summing. The mean still equals the total divided by the count of values.

questions.question: Find the median of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70.
questions.explanation: With seven ordered values, the middle (fourth) value is 40.
questions.theory: For an odd number of ordered values, the median is the single central value. Its position is the (n+1)/2-th value.

questions.question: Find the mode of 12, 15, 12, 18, 12, 15.
questions.explanation: The value 12 appears three times, more than 15 (twice) or 18 (once).
questions.theory: The mode is the value occurring most often. Counting occurrences of each value reveals which appears most frequently.

questions.question: Find the range of 3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2.
questions.explanation: The range is the largest value (9) minus the smallest value (1), which equals 8.
questions.theory: The range depends only on the maximum and minimum, ignoring all values in between. Identify the highest and lowest, then subtract.

questions.question: Find the mean of 8, 12.
questions.explanation: The sum is 20, and 20 divided by 2 values equals 10.
questions.theory: The mean of two numbers is their sum divided by two, which is their midpoint. The mean always lies between the two values.

questions.question: Find the median of 6, 6, 6, 6, 6.
questions.explanation: All five values are 6, so the middle value is 6.
questions.theory: When every value is the same, the median equals that value. The median is simply the central value of the ordered data.

questions.question: Find the mode of 2, 4, 6, 8, 8, 8, 8.
questions.explanation: The value 8 appears four times, more than any other value.
questions.theory: The mode is the most frequent value in the data set. The value with the greatest count of occurrences is the mode.

questions.question: Find the range of 45, 45, 50, 55.
questions.explanation: The range is 55 minus 45, which equals 10.
questions.theory: The range is the maximum minus the minimum. Repeated values do not change the range, only the extremes matter.

questions.question: Find the mean of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
questions.explanation: The sum is 20, and 20 divided by 5 values equals 4.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum of the values divided by their count. For consecutive integers, the mean is the middle value.

questions.question: Find the median of 14, 10, 12.
questions.explanation: Ordered, the values are 10, 12, 14, and the middle value is 12.
questions.theory: The median is the middle value after sorting. With three numbers, it is the second one in order.

questions.question: Find the mode of 5, 10, 15, 20, 20.
questions.explanation: The value 20 appears twice while all others appear once.
questions.theory: The mode is the value that repeats most often. Here only 20 repeats, making it the mode.

questions.question: True or False: To find the median, you must first put the numbers in order.
questions.explanation: The median is the middle value of ordered data, so sorting is required first.
questions.theory: The median is defined as the central value of a sorted data set. Skipping the ordering step usually gives the wrong answer.

questions.question: Find the mean of 1, 1, 1, 5.
questions.explanation: The sum is 8, and 8 divided by 4 values equals 2.
questions.theory: The mean accounts for every value, including repeats. It is the total divided by the number of values.

questions.question: Find the median of 100, 200, 300, 400.
questions.explanation: With four values, the median is the average of the two middle values, (200+300)/2 = 250.
questions.theory: For an even number of values, the median is the mean of the two central values. Order the data, then average the middle pair.

questions.question: Find the range of 0, 100.
questions.explanation: The range is 100 minus 0, which equals 100.
questions.theory: The range is the maximum value minus the minimum value. Subtracting the smallest from the largest gives the spread.

questions.question: Find the mode of 9, 9, 9, 9.
questions.explanation: Every value is 9, so the mode is 9.
questions.theory: The mode is the most frequently occurring value. When all values are identical, that value is the mode.

questions.question: Find the mean of 7, 14, 21.
questions.explanation: The sum is 42, and 42 divided by 3 values equals 14.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum divided by the count of values. For three evenly spaced numbers, it equals the middle value.

questions.question: Find the median of 8, 3, 9, 1, 6, 4.
questions.explanation: Ordered, the values are 1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, and the median is (4+6)/2 = 5.
questions.theory: With an even count of values, sort the data and average the two middle numbers. The result may not appear in the original set.

questions.question: Find the mode of 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3.
questions.explanation: The value 3 appears three times, more than 1 or 2, which each appear twice.
questions.theory: The mode is the value with the greatest frequency. Even when several values repeat, the one repeating most is the mode.

questions.question: Find the range of 18, 6, 24, 12.
questions.explanation: The range is 24 minus 6, which equals 18.
questions.theory: The range is the difference between the largest and smallest values. Only the extremes are used in the calculation.

questions.question: Find the mean of 4, 4, 6, 6.
questions.explanation: The sum is 20, and 20 divided by 4 values equals 5.
questions.theory: The mean is the total of all values divided by the number of values. Repeated values are each counted in the sum.

questions.question: Find the median of 11, 13, 15, 17, 19.
questions.explanation: With five ordered values, the middle (third) value is 15.
questions.theory: For an odd number of ordered values, the median is the single value in the center. The data here is already sorted.

questions.question: True or False: The range tells you how spread out a set of data is.
questions.explanation: The range measures spread as the difference between the highest and lowest values.
questions.theory: The range is a measure of dispersion (spread), not central tendency. A larger range means the values are more spread out.

questions.question: Find the mean of 6, 7, 8, 9, 10.
questions.explanation: The sum is 40, and 40 divided by 5 values equals 8.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum of the values divided by their count. For consecutive integers, it equals the middle value.

questions.question: Find the median of 5, 9, 1, 7.
questions.explanation: Ordered, the values are 1, 5, 7, 9, and the median is (5+7)/2 = 6.
questions.theory: For four values, sort them and average the two middle numbers to get the median. Ordering first is essential.

questions.question: Find the mode of 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 6, 7.
questions.explanation: The value 5 appears three times, more than 6 (twice) or any other value.
questions.theory: The mode is the value that occurs most frequently. Counting each value's occurrences identifies the most common one.

questions.question: Find the range of 33, 27, 41, 30, 25.
questions.explanation: The range is the largest value (41) minus the smallest value (25), which equals 16.
questions.theory: The range uses only the maximum and minimum values of a data set. Find the highest and lowest, then subtract.

questions.question: Find the mean of 10, 10, 20, 20, 40.
questions.explanation: The sum is 100, and 100 divided by 5 values equals 20.
questions.theory: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the number of values. Every value, including repeats, contributes to the total.

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 12. Four of them are 10, 11, 13, and 14. What is the fifth number?
questions.explanation: The total must be 5 x 12 = 60, and the four known values sum to 48, so the fifth is 60 - 48 = 12.
questions.theory: The mean equals the sum of all values divided by the count. If you know the mean and count, the total is fixed, so a missing value is the total minus the sum of the known values.

questions.question: A data set of yearly incomes includes one billionaire among mostly modest earners. Which measure best represents a typical income?
options: The mode, because it is always the largest
The median, because it resists the extreme value
The range, because it shows the spread
The mean, because it uses every value
questions.explanation: A single extreme high value pulls the mean upward, while the median stays near the bulk of the data and better reflects a typical income.
questions.theory: The median is the middle value of ordered data and is resistant to outliers. The mean uses every value, so a single extreme observation can drag it far from the center of the data.

questions.question: For the data set 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, what is the interquartile range (IQR)?
questions.explanation: With the five ordered values, the lower quartile is 6 and the upper quartile is 10, so the IQR is 10 - 6 = 4.
questions.theory: The interquartile range is Q3 minus Q1, the spread of the middle 50% of the data. Quartiles split ordered data into four equal parts.

questions.question: A student scores 80 on a test worth 70% of the grade and 90 on a test worth 30%. What is the weighted average?
questions.explanation: The weighted average is 0.70 x 80 + 0.30 x 90 = 56 + 27 = 83.
questions.theory: A weighted average multiplies each value by its weight and sums the results, where the weights total 1 (or 100%). Values with larger weights influence the average more.

questions.question: Adding an outlier that is much larger than the rest of the data generally affects the mean more than the median.
questions.explanation: The mean incorporates the outlier's actual magnitude, while the median only depends on position, so the mean shifts more.
questions.theory: The mean is sensitive to every value's size, so extreme values move it substantially. The median depends only on the middle position and is resistant to outliers.

questions.question: The mean of 6 numbers is 15. If one number that equals 15 is removed, what is the mean of the remaining 5 numbers?
questions.explanation: Removing a value equal to the mean leaves the total reduced by 15 and the count by 1, so the mean stays 90 - 15 = 75 over 5 = 15.
questions.theory: Removing a value equal to the current mean does not change the mean, because the remaining sum and count shrink proportionally. The mean only changes when removed values differ from it.

questions.question: Set A is 2, 4, 6 and Set B is 1, 4, 7. Both have the same mean. Which set is more spread out?
questions.explanation: Both have mean 4, but Set B ranges from 1 to 7 (range 6) while Set A ranges from 2 to 6 (range 4), so B is more spread out.
questions.theory: Two data sets can share the same mean yet differ in spread. Measures like range and standard deviation describe how far values fall from the center.

questions.question: The mean of 4 numbers is 20. Three of them are 18, 22, and 25. What is the fourth number?
questions.explanation: The total is 4 x 20 = 80, and 18 + 22 + 25 = 65, so the fourth number is 80 - 65 = 15.
questions.theory: When the mean and count are known, the sum of all values is fixed. A single missing value equals that total minus the sum of the known values.

questions.question: Which statement about the median of an even-sized data set is correct?
options: It is always one of the data values
It is the largest of the middle values
It is the average of the two middle values
questions.explanation: For an even number of ordered values, the median is the mean of the two central values and need not be a data point.
questions.theory: The median is the middle of ordered data. With an even count there is no single middle, so the median is the average of the two middle values.

questions.question: A class of 20 students averages 70 on a quiz, and another class of 30 students averages 80. What is the combined mean for all 50 students?
questions.theory: To combine group means, weight each by its group size: the overall mean is the total of all values divided by the total count, not the simple average of the two means.

questions.question: For the ordered data 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 17, what is the lower quartile Q1?
questions.explanation: The lower half is 3, 5, 7, 9, whose median is the average of 5 and 7, giving Q1 = 6.
questions.theory: Q1 is the median of the lower half of ordered data. For an even-sized lower half, it is the average of that half's two middle values.

questions.question: The range of a data set is always greater than or equal to its interquartile range.
questions.explanation: The range spans the full data while the IQR spans only the middle 50%, so the range is at least as large.
questions.theory: The range is max minus min, covering all values. The IQR covers only the middle half, so it can never exceed the range.

questions.question: A shopkeeper sells items at 2, 3, 3, 4, and 100 dollars. Which measure of center is most misleading here?
questions.explanation: The value 100 inflates the mean to about 22, far above the four cheap items, so the mean misrepresents a typical price.
questions.theory: Outliers distort the mean because it sums actual magnitudes. When extreme values exist, the mean can lie far from where most data sit, making it misleading.

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 9. If every number is increased by 4, what is the new mean?
questions.explanation: Adding 4 to each value adds 4 to the mean, so the new mean is 9 + 4 = 13.
questions.theory: Adding a constant to every value shifts the mean by that same constant. The spread (range, IQR, standard deviation) is unchanged by a constant shift.

questions.question: Two students have test averages of 85 over 4 tests and 75 over 6 tests. What is their combined average over all 10 tests?
questions.theory: Combining averages requires weighting by the number of items in each group. The combined mean is the grand total divided by the total count.

questions.question: For the data 12, 15, 15, 18, 20, which is true about the mode and median?
questions.explanation: The value 15 appears most often (mode = 15) and is also the middle of the five ordered values (median = 15).
questions.theory: The mode is the most frequent value; the median is the middle of ordered data. They can coincide but are defined differently.

questions.question: The mean of 7 numbers is 10. Six of them sum to 65. What is the seventh number?
questions.explanation: The total is 7 x 10 = 70, so the seventh number is 70 - 65 = 5.
questions.theory: With a known mean and count, the total sum is fixed. A missing value equals the total minus the sum of the known values.

questions.question: A symmetric, bell-shaped data set has no outliers. How do its mean and median compare?
questions.explanation: In a symmetric distribution the mean and median fall at the same central point, so they are approximately equal.
questions.theory: For symmetric distributions, the mean and median coincide. Skewness pulls the mean toward the longer tail, separating it from the median.

questions.question: In a right-skewed (positively skewed) data set, which is typically true?
options: The mode is the largest value
The mean is greater than the median
The mean is less than the median
questions.explanation: A long right tail pulls the mean above the median, so the mean exceeds the median.
questions.theory: Skewness shifts the mean toward the longer tail. In right-skewed data the tail is on the high side, so the mean lands above the median.

questions.question: A worker earns 15 dollars/hour for 30 hours and 20 dollars/hour for 10 hours. What is the average hourly rate over all hours worked?
questions.explanation: Total pay is 30 x 15 + 10 x 20 = 450 + 200 = 650 over 40 hours, giving 650 / 40 = 16.25.
questions.theory: An average rate is total amount divided by total units, a weighted average where hours are the weights. It is not the simple average of the two rates.

questions.question: The median of 5, 8, x, 14, 17 (already in order) is 11. What is x?
questions.explanation: With five ordered values the median is the third value, so x = 11.
questions.theory: For an odd-sized ordered list, the median is the single middle value. Its position is fixed by the count, so the middle entry equals the median.

questions.question: Multiplying every value in a data set by 3 multiplies the range by 3.
questions.explanation: Scaling all values by 3 scales both the max and min by 3, so their difference (the range) is also multiplied by 3.
questions.theory: Multiplying data by a constant scales measures of spread (range, IQR, standard deviation) by that constant. Measures of center scale by the same factor.

questions.question: Set A is 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and Set B is 28, 29, 30, 31, 32. Which has the larger standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Both center on 30, but Set A's values lie much farther from the mean than Set B's, so Set A has the larger standard deviation.
questions.theory: Standard deviation measures typical distance from the mean. Data clustered tightly around the mean has a small standard deviation; widely scattered data has a large one.

questions.question: The mean of 8 numbers is 11. If a ninth number, 20, is added, what is the new mean?
questions.explanation: The original total is 8 x 11 = 88; adding 20 gives 108 over 9 values, so 108 / 9 = 12.
questions.theory: To update a mean when adding a value, add the new value to the total and increase the count by one, then divide. A value above the mean raises it.

questions.question: Which measure of spread is most resistant to outliers?
questions.explanation: The IQR uses only the middle 50% of data, ignoring extreme values, so it resists outliers better than range, variance, or standard deviation.
questions.theory: Range, variance, and standard deviation all depend on extreme values. The IQR depends only on the quartiles, making it robust against outliers.

questions.question: A teacher averages four grade categories: 60, 70, 80, and 90, weighted 10%, 20%, 30%, and 40% respectively. What is the weighted average?
questions.theory: A weighted average multiplies each value by its weight and adds the products, with weights summing to 1. Heavier weights pull the result toward those values.

questions.question: For the data set 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, what is the interquartile range?
questions.explanation: The lower half median is Q1 = 9 and the upper half median is Q3 = 18, so the IQR is 18 - 9 = 9.
questions.theory: For an even-sized data set, split it into lower and upper halves; Q1 and Q3 are the medians of those halves, and the IQR is Q3 minus Q1.

questions.question: A data set has mean 50 and a single value is changed from 50 to 90. What happens to the mean?
questions.explanation: Replacing a value with a larger one increases the total, so the mean rises.
questions.theory: The mean responds to every change in the data. Increasing any single value raises the total and therefore raises the mean.

questions.question: The five-number summary of a data set is min 4, Q1 10, median 14, Q3 22, max 30. What is the IQR?
questions.explanation: The IQR is Q3 - Q1 = 22 - 10 = 12.
questions.theory: A five-number summary lists the minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum. The IQR is computed directly as Q3 minus Q1.

questions.question: Set A is 5, 5, 5, 5 and Set B is 2, 4, 6, 8. Both have mean 5. What is the standard deviation of Set A?
questions.explanation: Every value in Set A equals the mean, so all deviations are 0 and the standard deviation is 0.
questions.theory: Standard deviation measures spread around the mean. If all values are identical, there is no spread, so the standard deviation is exactly 0.

questions.question: The mean age of 3 children is 8. A fourth child aged 12 joins. What is the new mean age?
questions.explanation: The original total age is 3 x 8 = 24; adding 12 gives 36 over 4 children, so 36 / 4 = 9.
questions.theory: Adding a new value updates the mean by adding it to the total and dividing by the new count. A value above the old mean raises it.

questions.question: Which scenario most justifies reporting the median instead of the mean?
options: Dice rolls from a fair die
House prices in a city with a few mansions
Daily temperatures over a calm week
Heights of students in one class
questions.explanation: A few very expensive homes create extreme values that inflate the mean, so the median better represents a typical house price.
questions.theory: The median is preferred when data are skewed or contain outliers, since it is not distorted by extreme values the way the mean is.

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 14, and the mean of a different set of 3 numbers is 22. What is the mean of all 8 numbers combined?
questions.explanation: Totals are 5 x 14 = 70 and 3 x 22 = 66, summing to 136 over 8 values, so 136 / 8 = 17.
questions.theory: To combine the means of two groups, add their totals and divide by the combined count. This weights each group by its size.

questions.question: If a data set is symmetric, the mean and median are equal.
questions.explanation: Symmetry places the balance point (mean) and the middle value (median) at the same location.
questions.theory: In a perfectly symmetric distribution, values mirror around the center, so the mean and median coincide. Asymmetry separates them.

questions.question: For the data 7, 7, 8, 9, 10, 10, 10, 14, what is the mode?
questions.explanation: The value 10 appears three times, more than any other, so the mode is 10.
questions.theory: The mode is the value that occurs most frequently. A data set can have one mode, several modes, or none if all values are unique.

questions.question: A car travels 100 km at 50 km/h and another 100 km at 100 km/h. What is the average speed for the whole trip?
questions.explanation: Total distance is 200 km and total time is 2 + 1 = 3 hours, so the average speed is 200 / 3 = 66.7 km/h.
questions.theory: Average speed is total distance divided by total time, not the simple average of speeds. Equal distances at different speeds spend more time at the slower speed, pulling the average down.

questions.question: The mean of 6 numbers is 10. One value is mistakenly recorded as 8 instead of the correct 20. What is the correct mean?
questions.explanation: The recorded total is 60; correcting 8 to 20 adds 12, giving 72 over 6 values, so 72 / 6 = 12.
questions.theory: Correcting a data error changes the total by the difference between the wrong and right values. Recompute the mean from the corrected total.

questions.question: Comparing two classes with the same mean score, Class X has IQR 6 and Class Y has IQR 14. What does this indicate?
options: Class Y has a higher mean
Class Y's scores are more spread out
Class X's scores are more spread out
questions.explanation: A larger IQR means the middle 50% of scores covers a wider range, so Class Y's scores are more spread out.
questions.theory: The IQR measures the spread of the central half of the data. With equal means, a larger IQR signals greater variability among typical values.

questions.question: The mean of 4 numbers is 25. If three of them are 20, 30, and 30, what is the fourth?
questions.explanation: The total is 4 x 25 = 100, and 20 + 30 + 30 = 80, so the fourth number is 100 - 80 = 20.
questions.theory: With a fixed mean and count, the sum is determined. A single unknown value equals the total minus the sum of the known values.

questions.question: In a data set, the mean is 40 and the median is 55. What does this suggest about the shape?
questions.explanation: When the mean is below the median, a long left tail is pulling the mean down, indicating left skew.
questions.theory: The mean is pulled toward the longer tail. If the mean is less than the median, the tail extends to the left, so the data are left-skewed.

questions.question: A portfolio invests 60% in a fund returning 5% and 40% in a fund returning 10%. What is the overall return?
questions.explanation: The weighted return is 0.6 x 5% + 0.4 x 10% = 3% + 4% = 7%.
questions.theory: A portfolio return is a weighted average of component returns, weighted by the fraction invested in each. The weights sum to 100%.

questions.question: For the eight ordered values 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, what is the median?
questions.explanation: With eight values the median is the average of the 4th and 5th, (8 + 10) / 2 = 9.
questions.theory: For an even number of ordered values, the median is the mean of the two central values, here the fourth and fifth entries.

questions.question: Removing the single largest value from a right-skewed data set will most likely do what to the mean?
questions.explanation: The largest value sits above the mean in right-skewed data, so removing it lowers the total and decreases the mean.
questions.theory: The mean reflects every value. Removing a high outlier reduces the total disproportionately, pulling the mean down toward the remaining data.

questions.question: The mean weight of 10 boxes is 12 kg. If the total weight is later found to be 130 kg, what is the corrected mean?
questions.explanation: The corrected mean is the corrected total divided by the count: 130 / 10 = 13 kg.
questions.theory: The mean is total divided by count. If the total is revised, recompute the mean by dividing the new total by the unchanged count.

questions.question: The mean of a data set must always be one of the actual data values.
questions.explanation: The mean is an average and frequently lands between values, as with the mean of 2 and 3 being 2.5, which is not in the set.
questions.theory: The mean is a computed balance point and need not equal any observed value. The mode, by contrast, is always an actual data value.

questions.question: Two data sets have the same range but Set P has values bunched near the center while Set Q spreads evenly. Which likely has the smaller standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Values bunched near the mean have smaller deviations, so Set P has the smaller standard deviation despite equal ranges.
questions.theory: Standard deviation depends on how far all values lie from the mean, not just the extremes. Two sets can share a range yet differ in standard deviation based on clustering.

questions.question: The mean of 5 test scores is 78. After a 6th test, the mean rises to 80. What did the student score on the 6th test?
questions.explanation: The old total is 5 x 78 = 390 and the new total is 6 x 80 = 480, so the 6th score is 480 - 390 = 90.
questions.theory: To find a new value that changes a mean, compute the old and new totals from the means and counts; the new value is the difference between them.

questions.question: A grade is 40% homework (average 90) and 60% exams (average 70). What is the final weighted grade?
questions.explanation: The weighted grade is 0.4 x 90 + 0.6 x 70 = 36 + 42 = 78.
questions.theory: A weighted course grade multiplies each component average by its weight and sums them. Components with greater weight have more influence on the final grade.

questions.question: For the data 5, 10, 15, 20, 100, which statement is most accurate?
options: The mean and median are equal
The mode is the best measure of center
The median is larger than the mean
The median (15) better reflects the center than the mean (30)
questions.explanation: The value 100 inflates the mean to 30, while the median of 15 stays near the bulk of the data and better reflects the center.
questions.theory: When an outlier is present, the mean is pulled toward it while the median remains stable. The median then gives a more representative center.

questions.question: The mean of 9 numbers is 6. If three more numbers with mean 14 are added, what is the mean of all 12 numbers?
questions.explanation: Totals are 9 x 6 = 54 and 3 x 14 = 42, summing to 96 over 12 values, so 96 / 12 = 8.
questions.theory: To merge two groups, add their totals and divide by the total count. The combined mean is weighted by each group's size.

questions.question: Class A has 20 students with a mean score of 70; Class B has 30 students with a mean score of 80. What is the combined mean of all 50 students?
questions.theory: A combined (weighted) mean is the total of all values divided by the total count, not the simple average of the group means. Each group mean is weighted by its sample size.

questions.question: A group of 4 numbers has a mean of 10. Three of the numbers are 8, 9, and 12. What is the fourth number?
questions.explanation: The total must be 4*10 = 40, and 8 + 9 + 12 = 29, so the fourth number is 40 - 29 = 11.
questions.theory: If the mean is known, the sum equals mean times count. A missing value can be recovered by subtracting the known values from that required sum.

questions.question: If every value in a data set is increased by 5, which of the following is unchanged?
questions.explanation: Adding a constant shifts the mean, median, and maximum each up by 5 but leaves the spread (standard deviation) unchanged.
questions.theory: Adding a constant to every value is a rigid shift: all measures of center and position move by that constant, while measures of spread (range, IQR, variance, SD) stay the same because distances between points do not change.

questions.question: If every value in a data set is multiplied by 3, what happens to the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Scaling every value by a factor multiplies the standard deviation by the absolute value of that factor, so SD becomes 3 times as large.
questions.theory: Standard deviation scales linearly with multiplicative scaling of the data, while variance (the square of SD) scales by the square of the factor. Both are measured in the data's units, so they respond to scaling.

questions.question: If every value in a data set is multiplied by 4, what happens to the variance?
questions.explanation: Variance scales by the square of the multiplier, so multiplying values by 4 multiplies the variance by 4^2 = 16.
questions.theory: Variance is the average of squared deviations, so a multiplicative factor k applied to the data multiplies each squared deviation by k^2, scaling the variance by k^2.

questions.question: A distribution is right-skewed (positively skewed). Which relationship between the mean and median is most typical?
options: Mean and median cannot be compared
questions.explanation: In a right-skewed distribution the long tail of large values pulls the mean above the median.
questions.theory: Skew describes the direction of a distribution's longer tail. Because the mean is sensitive to extreme values and the median is not, the mean is pulled toward the tail: above the median for right skew, below it for left skew.

questions.question: Which of these data sets has the largest standard deviation?
questions.explanation: The set {1, 5, 15, 19} has values spread farthest from its mean of 10, giving it the largest standard deviation.
questions.theory: Standard deviation measures how far values typically lie from the mean. Sets with values clustered tightly have small SD; sets with values spread widely from the center have large SD.

questions.question: True or False: Adding a constant to every value in a data set changes its variance.
questions.explanation: Adding a constant shifts all values equally, so deviations from the mean are unchanged and the variance stays the same.
questions.theory: Variance depends only on the distances of values from their mean. A constant shift moves every value and the mean by the same amount, leaving all deviations and therefore the variance unchanged.

questions.question: The variance of the data set {2, 4, 6, 8} using the population formula (dividing by N) is:
questions.explanation: The mean is 5; squared deviations are 9, 1, 1, 9 summing to 20, and 20/4 = 5.
questions.theory: Population variance is the mean of squared deviations from the mean: compute each value minus the mean, square it, then average over all N values.

questions.question: A data set has population variance 36. What is its standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Standard deviation is the square root of variance, and the square root of 36 is 6.
questions.theory: Standard deviation is defined as the non-negative square root of the variance. It returns the spread to the original units of the data, whereas variance is in squared units.

questions.question: Which single change to the data set {3, 5, 7, 9, 11} would most increase its mean while leaving its median unchanged?
questions.explanation: Replacing the largest value 11 with 100 raises the sum (and mean) sharply but keeps the middle value 7 as the median.
questions.theory: The median depends only on the middle position in ordered data, so changing an extreme value does not move it. The mean uses every value, so changing an extreme value can shift it substantially.

questions.question: Two groups are combined. Group 1 has 10 values with mean 50; Group 2 has 40 values with mean 50. What is the combined mean?
questions.explanation: When both groups share the same mean of 50, the combined mean is also 50 regardless of the group sizes.
questions.theory: A weighted mean lies between the group means. When all groups have identical means, the weighted average equals that common value no matter how the weights are distributed.

questions.question: Which measure of center is most resistant to the influence of a single extreme outlier?
questions.explanation: The median depends only on the middle of the ordered data, so a single extreme value barely affects it.
questions.theory: Robustness refers to how little a statistic changes when outliers are present. The median is robust because it ignores the magnitude of extreme values, while the mean is non-robust because every value contributes to it.

questions.question: A data set of 5 values has a mean of 12. If one value of 20 is removed, what is the mean of the remaining 4 values?
questions.explanation: The original total is 5*12 = 60; removing 20 leaves 40, and 40/4 = 10.
questions.theory: Removing a value above the mean lowers the mean of what remains. Compute the original sum, subtract the removed value, and divide by the new count.

questions.question: For the data set {4, 4, 4, 4, 4}, what is the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: All values are identical, so every deviation from the mean is zero and the standard deviation is 0.
questions.theory: Standard deviation is zero exactly when there is no variability: every value equals the mean. Any difference among values produces a positive standard deviation.

questions.question: True or False: In a perfectly symmetric distribution, the mean and median are equal.
questions.explanation: Symmetry balances values on both sides of the center, so the mean and median coincide.
questions.theory: In a symmetric distribution, values are mirror images around the center point, so the balancing point (mean) and the middle point (median) fall at the same location.

questions.question: The range of a data set is 30. If every value is multiplied by 2, the new range is:
questions.explanation: Multiplying all values by 2 doubles the gap between the maximum and minimum, so the range becomes 60.
questions.theory: The range is the difference between the largest and smallest values. Scaling every value by a factor multiplies that difference by the same factor, so range scales like SD does.

questions.question: A factory's daily output averages 200 units over 6 days. To raise the 7-day average to 210, how many units must be produced on day 7?
questions.explanation: Seven days at 210 require 1470 units; six days produced 1200, so day 7 needs 1470 - 1200 = 270.
questions.theory: To hit a target average, compute the total required (target times count) and subtract what has already accumulated. The shortfall is the needed value for the remaining item.

questions.question: Set X = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} and Set Y = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}. Which statement is correct?
options: X has a larger mean than Y
They have the same mean but X has a larger standard deviation
They have the same mean but Y has a larger standard deviation
They have the same mean and the same standard deviation
questions.explanation: Both sets have a mean of 6, but X's values spread farther from 6, giving it the larger standard deviation.
questions.theory: Two data sets can share a mean yet differ in spread. The set whose values lie farther from the common mean has the greater standard deviation, even though their centers match.

questions.question: A salary data set is strongly right-skewed. Which average best represents a 'typical' worker's pay?
options: The range, because it shows the spread
The mean, because it uses all data
The median, because it resists the high outliers
The maximum, because it shows the ceiling
questions.explanation: With right-skewed pay, a few very high salaries inflate the mean, so the median better reflects a typical worker.
questions.theory: For skewed data, the median is usually a more representative measure of center because the mean is dragged toward the tail. This is why income figures are commonly reported as medians.

questions.question: The mean of 6 numbers is 15. If each number is increased by 4, the new mean is:
questions.explanation: Adding 4 to every value adds 4 to the mean, giving 15 + 4 = 19.
questions.theory: Adding a constant c to every value increases the mean by exactly c, because the total rises by c times the count and dividing by the count restores the per-item increase.

questions.question: The mean of 8 numbers is 10. If each number is multiplied by 5, the new mean is:
questions.explanation: Multiplying every value by 5 multiplies the mean by 5, giving 10 * 5 = 50.
questions.theory: Scaling every value by a factor k multiplies the mean by k, since the sum scales by k and the count is unchanged. The mean responds to both shifting and scaling.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of statistics are BOTH unaffected by adding a constant to every data value?
questions.explanation: Range and standard deviation both measure spread, which a constant shift leaves unchanged, while mean, median, and maximum all shift.
questions.theory: Measures of spread (range, IQR, variance, standard deviation) are invariant under adding a constant, whereas measures of location (mean, median, min, max, quartiles) all shift by that constant.

questions.question: A data set {10, 12, 14, 16, 18} has its largest value 18 changed to 38. How does the median change?
questions.explanation: Changing only the largest value does not move the middle value, so the median remains 14.
questions.theory: The median is determined by position, not magnitude, in ordered data. Altering a value that stays at an end of the ordering leaves the central position, and thus the median, unchanged.

questions.question: Team P has 5 players averaging 80 kg; Team Q has 15 players averaging 72 kg. The combined average weight is:
questions.explanation: Combined total is 5*80 + 15*72 = 400 + 1080 = 1480 kg over 20 players, giving 1480/20 = 74 kg.
questions.theory: The weighted mean of two groups is their combined total divided by their combined count. The result is pulled toward the larger group's mean.

questions.question: True or False: A data set can have a standard deviation that is negative.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation is a square root of an average of squares, so it is always zero or positive, never negative.
questions.theory: Standard deviation is defined as the non-negative square root of variance, and variance is an average of squared (hence non-negative) deviations. Therefore SD cannot be negative.

questions.question: The population variance of {5, 5, 5, 9} is:
questions.explanation: The mean is 6; squared deviations are 1, 1, 1, 9 summing to 12, and 12/4 = 3.
questions.theory: Population variance averages squared deviations from the mean over all N values. Repeated values close to the mean contribute little, while values far from the mean dominate the variance.

questions.question: In a left-skewed (negatively skewed) distribution, which is typically true?
questions.explanation: A left-skewed distribution has a long tail of small values that pulls the mean below the median.
questions.theory: Left (negative) skew means the longer tail points toward smaller values. The mean is dragged toward that low tail, so it falls below the median, the reverse of right-skewed behavior.

questions.question: A student scores 60, 70, and 80 on three equally weighted tests, then 90 on a final worth double a normal test. What is the weighted average across all five test-weights?
questions.explanation: With weights 1,1,1,2 the total is 60+70+80+2*90 = 390 over 5 weight units, giving 390/5 = 78.
questions.theory: A weighted average multiplies each value by its weight, sums those products, and divides by the total of the weights. Higher-weight items influence the result more than lower-weight ones.

questions.question: Set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and Set B = {1, 1, 1, 9, 9}. Both have a mean of 3. Which has the greater spread?
questions.explanation: Set B's values lie much farther from the mean of 3 than Set A's, so Set B has the greater spread.
questions.theory: Spread reflects how far values deviate from the center. Even with equal means, a set with values bunched at the extremes has larger deviations, hence greater variance and standard deviation.

questions.question: The mean of a data set equals its median, and the data is unimodal. This most strongly suggests the distribution is:
questions.explanation: When the mean and median coincide in a unimodal data set, the distribution is approximately symmetric.
questions.theory: The gap between mean and median signals skew direction and degree. When they are equal, neither tail dominates, indicating a roughly symmetric shape.

questions.question: A data set has mean 50 and standard deviation 10. If every value is transformed by the rule (value - 50)/10, what are the new mean and standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Subtracting 50 makes the mean 0, and dividing by 10 makes the standard deviation 1.
questions.theory: Standardizing (z-scoring) subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation, producing a data set with mean 0 and standard deviation 1. Shifting moves the center; scaling adjusts the spread.

questions.question: Adding one new value equal to the current mean to a data set will:
questions.explanation: A new value equal to the mean keeps the average balanced, so the mean does not change.
questions.theory: The mean is unchanged by adding a value exactly equal to it, because the total and count both grow in the proportion that preserves the average. Adding values above or below the mean would shift it.

questions.question: A data set of 10 values has a mean of 6. A new 11th value is added and the mean rises to 7. What was the added value?
questions.explanation: The new total must be 11*7 = 77 and the old total was 10*6 = 60, so the added value is 77 - 60 = 17.
questions.theory: When adding a value changes the mean, the added value equals the new required total minus the old total. The value must exceed the old mean to raise it.

questions.question: True or False: If two data sets have the same range, they must have the same standard deviation.
questions.explanation: Range depends only on the two extremes, while standard deviation depends on every value, so equal ranges can have different SDs.
questions.theory: The range uses only the minimum and maximum, ignoring the distribution of values between them. Two sets with identical extremes can differ greatly in how their interior values cluster, giving different standard deviations.

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 14. Four of them are 10, 12, 16, and 18. What is the fifth number?
questions.explanation: The total must be 5*14 = 70, and 10 + 12 + 16 + 18 = 56, so the fifth number is 70 - 56 = 14.
questions.theory: With a known mean, the sum is fixed at mean times count. A single unknown value is the difference between that required sum and the sum of the known values.

questions.question: Which transformation leaves the coefficient of variation (SD divided by mean) of a positive data set unchanged?
options: Multiplying every value by a positive constant
Subtracting a positive constant from every value
Adding a positive constant to every value
questions.explanation: Multiplying by a positive constant scales both the SD and the mean by the same factor, so their ratio is unchanged.
questions.theory: The coefficient of variation is a relative measure of spread. Multiplicative scaling affects numerator and denominator equally and cancels, but additive shifts change the mean without changing the SD, altering the ratio.

questions.question: A data set is {2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9}. Replacing the 9 with 90 would most affect which statistic the least?
questions.explanation: The median stays at the average of the two middle values 4 and 5 (which is 4.5), so it is least affected by changing the extreme 9.
questions.theory: Outliers strongly influence the mean, range, and standard deviation because those use magnitudes or extremes, but the median is positional and largely immune to a single extreme change.

questions.question: Group 1: 8 values, mean 90. Group 2: 12 values, mean 65. The combined mean is:
questions.theory: The combined mean weights each group's mean by its count. Because the larger group has the lower mean, the result is pulled below the simple midpoint of the two means.

questions.question: For a data set of all positive values, increasing the single largest value (keeping others fixed) will always:
questions.explanation: Raising the largest value increases the total, so dividing by the same count produces a larger mean.
questions.theory: Because the mean uses the sum of all values, increasing any value increases the total and therefore the mean. The median, by contrast, could stay fixed if the changed value remains at an extreme position.

questions.question: The standard deviation of a data set is 8. After subtracting 100 from every value, the new standard deviation is:
questions.explanation: Subtracting a constant shifts all values equally and does not change spread, so the standard deviation remains 8.
questions.theory: Subtracting a constant is an additive shift, which moves the center but preserves all pairwise distances. Measures of spread such as the standard deviation are therefore unchanged.

questions.question: A symmetric data set has mean 40. Its median must be:
questions.explanation: In a symmetric distribution the mean and median coincide, so the median equals 40.
questions.theory: Symmetry forces the mean and median to the same central location because values balance equally on both sides. Knowing one gives the other in a truly symmetric distribution.

questions.question: The population variance of {3, 7} is:
questions.explanation: The mean is 5; squared deviations are 4 and 4 summing to 8, and 8/2 = 4.
questions.theory: For two values, the population variance is the average of the two squared deviations from their mean. With values symmetric about the mean, both deviations are equal in magnitude.

questions.question: Which scenario would make the mean substantially larger than the median?
options: A few unusually large values in an otherwise low data set
A few unusually small values in an otherwise high data set
questions.explanation: A few very large values create a right skew that pulls the mean above the median.
questions.theory: The mean exceeds the median when the distribution is right-skewed, which happens when high outliers or a long upper tail inflate the average without affecting the positional median.

questions.question: A data set of 4 values has mean 25 and one value is incorrectly recorded. The recorded values are 20, 24, 28, and 28, but the last should be 36. What is the corrected mean?
questions.explanation: Correcting 28 to 36 adds 8 to the total of 100, giving 108, and 108/4 = 27.
questions.theory: Correcting one value changes the total by the size of the correction; the mean changes by that amount divided by the count. Here an increase of 8 over 4 values raises the mean by 2.

questions.question: Two data sets have the same mean. Set M has standard deviation 3 and Set N has standard deviation 9. Which statement is true?
options: Set N's values are more spread out
Set M's values are more spread out
questions.explanation: A larger standard deviation means values lie farther from the mean, so Set N is more spread out.
questions.theory: Standard deviation directly quantifies typical distance from the mean. With equal means, the set with the larger SD has values that vary more widely around that shared center.

questions.question: The mean age of 30 people is 40. Three people aged 30, 35, and 55 leave the group. What is the mean age of the remaining 27 people?
questions.explanation: Original total is 30*40 = 1200; removing 30+35+55 = 120 leaves 1080, and 1080/27 = 40.
questions.theory: Removing values whose own mean equals the overall mean leaves the mean unchanged. Here the departing trio averages 40, the same as the group, so the remaining mean stays 40.

questions.question: A data set has variance 0. Which statement must be true?
questions.explanation: Variance is zero only when every value equals the mean, meaning all values are identical.
questions.theory: Variance measures average squared deviation from the mean. It can be zero only if every deviation is zero, which requires all values to be equal; the common value can be any number, not necessarily 0.

questions.question: Mixture problem: 4 liters of solution averaging 10% concentration is mixed with 6 liters averaging 20%. The resulting concentration (a weighted mean) is:
questions.theory: A mixture's concentration is the volume-weighted mean of the component concentrations: weight each concentration by its volume, sum, and divide by the total volume. The result lands between the two inputs, closer to the larger volume.

questions.question: The mean of 9 numbers is 20. When a 10th number is added, the mean drops to 18. What was the added number?
questions.explanation: The new total is 10*18 = 180 and the old total was 9*20 = 180, so the added number is 180 - 180 = 0.
questions.theory: Adding a value below the current mean lowers it; the added value equals the new total minus the old total. A value of 0, well below 20, pulls the mean down as expected.

questions.question: You flip a fair coin once. What is the probability of getting heads?
questions.explanation: A fair coin has 2 equally likely outcomes, and 1 of them is heads, so the probability is 1/2.
questions.theory: Probability of a single event equals the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of equally likely outcomes. For a fair coin, each of the two sides has an equal chance.

questions.question: A standard six-sided die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a 4?
questions.explanation: There are 6 equally likely faces and only one shows a 4, so the probability is 1/6.
questions.theory: For a fair die, every face is equally likely. The probability of a specific number is 1 divided by the total number of faces, which is 6.

questions.question: A bag holds 3 red balls and 2 blue balls. If you draw one ball at random, what is the probability it is red?
questions.explanation: There are 5 balls total and 3 are red, so the probability of red is 3/5.
questions.theory: When all items are equally likely to be drawn, the probability of a color equals the count of that color divided by the total count of items.

questions.question: The probability of any event is always a number between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
questions.explanation: Probabilities range from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), so they are always between 0 and 1.
questions.theory: Probability is a measure of how likely an event is, scaled from 0 to 1. A value of 0 means it cannot happen and 1 means it is certain to happen.

questions.question: What is the probability expressed as a percent of getting tails on a single flip of a fair coin?
questions.explanation: The probability of tails is 1/2, which equals 50%.
questions.theory: A probability can be written as a fraction, a decimal, or a percent. To convert a fraction to a percent, divide and multiply by 100; 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%.

questions.question: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling an even number?
questions.explanation: The even faces are 2, 4, and 6 (3 outcomes) out of 6, so the probability is 3/6 = 1/2.
questions.theory: To find the probability of an event with several favorable outcomes, count those outcomes and divide by the total. Even numbers on a die are 2, 4, and 6.

questions.question: The probability that it will rain tomorrow is 0.3. What is the probability that it will NOT rain tomorrow?
questions.explanation: By the complement rule, the probability of not raining is 1 - 0.3 = 0.7.
questions.theory: The complement rule states that the probability an event does not happen equals 1 minus the probability that it does. The two probabilities always add up to 1.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a heart?
questions.explanation: There are 13 hearts out of 52 cards, so the probability is 13/52 = 1/4.
questions.theory: A standard deck has 52 cards split evenly into 4 suits of 13 cards each. The probability of drawing a given suit is 13/52, which simplifies to 1/4.

questions.question: What is the probability of an impossible event?
questions.explanation: An impossible event can never happen, so its probability is 0.
questions.theory: An impossible event is one with no favorable outcomes. Since favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes is 0, its probability is exactly 0.

questions.question: What is the probability of a certain event (one guaranteed to happen)?
questions.explanation: A certain event always happens, so its probability is 1.
questions.theory: A certain event includes every possible outcome. Its probability equals the total outcomes divided by the total outcomes, which is 1.

questions.question: A spinner is divided into 4 equal sections colored red, blue, green, and yellow. What is the probability the spinner lands on green?
questions.explanation: There are 4 equal sections and 1 is green, so the probability is 1/4.
questions.theory: When a spinner has equal-sized sections, each section is equally likely. The probability of landing on one specific color is 1 divided by the number of sections.

questions.question: How many possible outcomes are in the sample space when you roll one standard six-sided die?
questions.explanation: A single die can land on 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6, giving 6 possible outcomes.
questions.theory: The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. For one six-sided die, the sample space contains 6 outcomes.

questions.question: A bag contains 4 green marbles and 6 yellow marbles. What is the probability of drawing a yellow marble?
questions.explanation: There are 10 marbles total and 6 are yellow, so the probability is 6/10 = 3/5.
questions.theory: The probability of an outcome equals the number of favorable items divided by the total number of items. Here 6 yellow out of 10 total gives 6/10.

questions.question: Rolling a 7 on a single standard six-sided die is an impossible event.
questions.explanation: A standard die only has faces 1 through 6, so rolling a 7 cannot happen and is impossible.
questions.theory: An impossible event has no favorable outcomes in the sample space. Since 7 is not a face on a six-sided die, its probability is 0.

questions.question: What is the probability of rolling a number less than 7 on a single standard six-sided die?
questions.explanation: Every face (1-6) is less than 7, so the event is certain and the probability is 1.
questions.theory: An event that includes all outcomes in the sample space is certain, with probability 1. All six faces of the die are less than 7.

questions.question: Expressed as a decimal, what is the probability of rolling a 3 on a single standard six-sided die? (Rounded to two decimals.)
questions.explanation: The probability is 1/6, which equals about 0.17 when rounded to two decimals.
questions.theory: To convert a fraction to a decimal, divide the numerator by the denominator. 1 divided by 6 is approximately 0.1667, or 0.17 rounded.

questions.question: A jar has 5 white balls and 5 black balls. What is the probability of drawing a white ball?
questions.explanation: There are 10 balls total and 5 are white, so the probability is 5/10 = 1/2.
questions.theory: When favorable and total counts share a common factor, simplify the fraction. 5 white out of 10 balls reduces to 1/2.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is the ace of spades?
questions.explanation: There is exactly one ace of spades among 52 cards, so the probability is 1/52.
questions.theory: For a single specific card, there is only one favorable outcome out of 52 total cards. The probability is therefore 1/52.

questions.question: The probability of winning a game is 1/5. What is the probability of NOT winning?
questions.explanation: By the complement rule, the probability of not winning is 1 - 1/5 = 4/5.
questions.theory: The complement of an event covers all outcomes where the event does not occur. Subtract the event's probability from 1 to find it.

questions.question: How many outcomes are in the sample space when you flip a single coin once?
questions.explanation: A coin can land on heads or tails, giving 2 possible outcomes.
questions.theory: The sample space lists every possible result. For one coin flip, there are exactly two outcomes: heads and tails.

questions.question: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a number greater than 4?
questions.explanation: The numbers greater than 4 are 5 and 6 (2 outcomes) out of 6, so the probability is 2/6 = 1/3.
questions.theory: Count the outcomes that satisfy the condition and divide by the total. Faces greater than 4 are 5 and 6, giving 2 out of 6.

questions.question: A bag contains 1 red, 1 blue, 1 green, and 1 yellow ball. What is the probability of drawing the blue ball?
questions.explanation: There are 4 balls total and 1 is blue, so the probability is 1/4.
questions.theory: With distinct equally likely items, the probability of drawing a specific one is 1 divided by the total number of items.

questions.question: What is the probability written as a percent of rolling an even number on a single standard six-sided die?
questions.explanation: There are 3 even faces out of 6, so the probability is 3/6 = 1/2 = 50%.
questions.theory: Convert the fraction of favorable outcomes to a percent by multiplying by 100. Three even faces out of six gives 1/2, which is 50%.

questions.question: If the probability of an event is 1, the event is certain to happen.
questions.explanation: A probability of 1 means the event will definitely occur, so it is certain.
questions.theory: On the probability scale, 1 represents certainty. An event with probability 1 includes all outcomes in the sample space.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a face card (jack, queen, or king)?
questions.explanation: There are 12 face cards (3 per suit times 4 suits) out of 52, so the probability is 12/52 = 3/13.
questions.theory: Face cards are the jack, queen, and king in each of the four suits, totaling 12 cards. The probability is 12 out of 52.

questions.question: A spinner has 8 equal sections numbered 1 to 8. What is the probability of landing on the number 5?
questions.explanation: There are 8 equal sections and 1 shows the number 5, so the probability is 1/8.
questions.theory: For an evenly divided spinner, each section is equally likely. The probability of a single labeled section is 1 divided by the number of sections.

questions.question: A box has 7 apples and 3 oranges. What is the probability of randomly picking an orange?
questions.explanation: There are 10 pieces of fruit and 3 are oranges, so the probability is 3/10.
questions.theory: The probability of selecting a type of item equals the count of that type divided by the total number of items in the box.

questions.question: The probability of drawing a defective part is 0.1. What is the probability the part is NOT defective?
questions.explanation: By the complement rule, the probability of a non-defective part is 1 - 0.1 = 0.9.
questions.theory: The complement rule says probabilities of an event and its opposite sum to 1. Subtract 0.1 from 1 to get 0.9.

questions.question: What is the probability of rolling a 1 OR a 2 on a single standard six-sided die?
questions.explanation: Two faces (1 and 2) are favorable out of 6, so the probability is 2/6 = 1/3.
questions.theory: When an event allows more than one outcome, add up the favorable outcomes. Two favorable faces out of six gives 2/6, which simplifies to 1/3.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped. The probability of heads is 1/2. Using the complement rule, what is the probability of tails?
questions.explanation: The complement of heads is tails, so its probability is 1 - 1/2 = 1/2.
questions.theory: Heads and tails are complementary outcomes of a coin flip. Their probabilities add to 1, so each is 1/2 for a fair coin.

questions.question: A bag has 10 marbles, all of them blue. What is the probability of drawing a blue marble?
questions.explanation: Every marble is blue, so drawing blue is certain with probability 1.
questions.theory: If every item in the sample space satisfies the condition, the event is certain. A certain event has probability 1.

questions.question: A bag has 10 marbles, none of them red. What is the probability of drawing a red marble?
questions.explanation: There are no red marbles, so drawing red is impossible with probability 0.
questions.theory: If no item in the sample space satisfies the condition, the event is impossible. An impossible event has probability 0.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a red card?
questions.explanation: There are 26 red cards (hearts and diamonds) out of 52, so the probability is 26/52 = 1/2.
questions.theory: A deck has two red suits (hearts and diamonds) totaling 26 cards. The probability of a red card is 26 out of 52, which is 1/2.

questions.question: Expressed as a decimal, what is the probability of getting heads on a single fair coin flip?
questions.explanation: The probability of heads is 1/2, which equals 0.5 as a decimal.
questions.theory: A fraction becomes a decimal by dividing the numerator by the denominator. 1 divided by 2 equals 0.5.

questions.question: A spinner has 5 equal sections. What is the probability of landing on a section, expressed as a percent?
questions.explanation: Each of the 5 equal sections has probability 1/5 = 20%.
questions.theory: Each equal section of a spinner has probability 1 divided by the number of sections. 1/5 equals 0.20, or 20%.

questions.question: The probabilities of an event and its complement always add up to 1.
questions.explanation: An event and its complement cover all possibilities, so their probabilities sum to 1.
questions.theory: The complement of an event includes every outcome where the event does not occur. Together they account for the entire sample space, summing to 1.

questions.question: A bag contains 2 red, 3 blue, and 5 green balls. What is the probability of drawing a red ball?
questions.explanation: There are 10 balls total and 2 are red, so the probability is 2/10 = 1/5.
questions.theory: Add all items to find the total, then divide the favorable count by it. Here 2 red out of 10 total gives 2/10, which reduces to 1/5.

questions.question: How many possible outcomes are in the sample space when drawing one card from a standard 52-card deck?
questions.explanation: There are 52 different cards, so the sample space has 52 outcomes.
questions.theory: The sample space contains every possible result of the experiment. A standard deck has 52 distinct cards, so there are 52 outcomes.

questions.question: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling an odd number?
questions.explanation: The odd faces are 1, 3, and 5 (3 outcomes) out of 6, so the probability is 3/6 = 1/2.
questions.theory: Odd numbers on a die are 1, 3, and 5. With 3 favorable outcomes out of 6, the probability is 3/6, which simplifies to 1/2.

questions.question: The probability of an event is 3/4. What is the probability of its complement?
questions.explanation: By the complement rule, the complement's probability is 1 - 3/4 = 1/4.
questions.theory: To find a complement's probability, subtract the event's probability from 1. Here 1 minus 3/4 leaves 1/4.

questions.question: A bag has 6 red balls and 4 white balls. What is the probability of drawing a red ball, as a percent?
questions.explanation: There are 10 balls and 6 are red, so the probability is 6/10 = 60%.
questions.theory: Convert the fraction of favorable outcomes to a percent. 6 red out of 10 balls is 6/10 = 0.6 = 60%.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a club?
questions.explanation: There are 13 clubs out of 52 cards, so the probability is 13/52 = 1/4.
questions.theory: Clubs are one of the four suits, each containing 13 cards. The probability of drawing a club is 13 out of 52, which is 1/4.

questions.question: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a 2, 3, or 4?
questions.explanation: Three faces (2, 3, 4) are favorable out of 6, so the probability is 3/6 = 1/2.
questions.theory: Sum the favorable outcomes when an event allows several results. Three favorable faces out of six gives 3/6, which simplifies to 1/2.

questions.question: An event with probability 0.5 is more likely to happen than an event with probability 0.2.
questions.explanation: A larger probability means a more likely event, and 0.5 is greater than 0.2.
questions.theory: Probabilities let us compare likelihoods: the higher the number, the more likely the event. 0.5 is greater than 0.2, so it is more likely.

questions.question: A bag contains 8 balls numbered 1 to 8. What is the probability of drawing the ball numbered 8?
questions.explanation: There are 8 balls and only 1 is numbered 8, so the probability is 1/8.
questions.theory: With distinct equally likely items, the probability of a single specific item is 1 divided by the total number of items.

questions.question: The probability that a light bulb works is 0.95. What is the probability it does NOT work?
questions.explanation: By the complement rule, the probability it does not work is 1 - 0.95 = 0.05.
questions.theory: The complement rule gives the probability of the opposite outcome by subtracting from 1. Here 1 minus 0.95 equals 0.05.

questions.question: A spinner is divided into 3 equal sections: red, blue, and green. What is the probability of NOT landing on red?
questions.explanation: Two of the three sections are not red, so the probability is 2/3 (also 1 - 1/3).
questions.theory: The complement of landing on red is landing on any other section. With 2 of 3 sections not red, the probability is 2/3.

questions.question: You draw one card from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a king?
questions.explanation: There are 4 kings out of 52 cards, so the probability is 4/52 = 1/13.
questions.theory: There is one king in each of the four suits, giving 4 kings. The probability is 4 out of 52, which simplifies to 1/13.

questions.question: A bag has 12 candies: 4 mint, 5 fruit, and 3 chocolate. What is the probability of drawing a fruit candy?
questions.explanation: There are 12 candies and 5 are fruit, so the probability is 5/12.
questions.theory: The probability of a category equals its count divided by the total number of items. Here 5 fruit out of 12 candies gives 5/12.

questions.question: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of NOT rolling a 6?
questions.explanation: Five faces (1-5) are not a 6, so the probability is 5/6 (also 1 - 1/6).
questions.theory: The complement of rolling a 6 is rolling any other number. With 5 favorable faces out of 6, the probability is 5/6.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the probability of getting heads on both flips?
questions.explanation: For independent events, multiply: (1/2) x (1/2) = 1/4.
questions.theory: Two events are independent when the outcome of one does not affect the other. The probability that both occur is the product of their individual probabilities.

questions.question: You roll a fair six-sided die. What is the probability of rolling a 2 or a 5?
questions.explanation: These outcomes are mutually exclusive, so add: 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3.
questions.theory: Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time. The probability that either one occurs is the sum of their individual probabilities.

questions.question: A bag has 3 red and 2 blue marbles. You draw one, replace it, then draw again. What is the probability both draws are red?
questions.explanation: With replacement the draws are independent: (3/5) x (3/5) = 9/25.
questions.theory: When an item is replaced before the next draw, the two draws are independent because the bag's composition is unchanged. Multiply the probabilities of each draw.

questions.question: A standard deck has 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a card that is a heart or a club?
questions.explanation: Hearts and clubs are mutually exclusive suits: 13/52 + 13/52 = 26/52 = 1/2.
questions.theory: When two outcomes share no common cases, the probability of either occurring is the sum of their probabilities. Suits in a deck do not overlap.

questions.question: You flip a fair coin 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one tail?
questions.explanation: Use the complement: 1 - P(no tails) = 1 - (1/2)^3 = 1 - 1/8 = 7/8.
questions.theory: The probability of 'at least one' is often easiest as 1 minus the probability of 'none.' The complement covers all remaining outcomes.

questions.question: A single card is drawn from a standard deck. What is the probability it is a king or a heart?
questions.explanation: Overlapping events: 4/52 + 13/52 - 1/52 = 16/52, so it is 16/52 = 4/13... recompute: 4+13-1=16, giving 16/52.
questions.theory: For events that overlap, add the probabilities and subtract the shared cases counted twice. This is the addition rule for non-mutually-exclusive events.

questions.question: If two events are mutually exclusive, the probability that both occur at the same time is 0.
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive means the events cannot occur together, so their joint probability is 0.
questions.theory: Mutually exclusive (disjoint) events have no outcomes in common. Because they cannot both happen, the probability of their intersection is zero.

questions.question: A spinner has 4 equal sections numbered 1 to 4. You spin twice. What is the probability of getting a 3 on the first spin and an even number on the second?
questions.explanation: Independent spins: (1/4) x (2/4) = (1/4) x (1/2) = 1/8.
questions.theory: Successive spins are independent. Multiply the probability of the first outcome by the probability of the second to get the combined probability.

questions.question: The probability it rains today is 0.3. Assuming days are independent, what is the probability it does NOT rain on two consecutive days?
questions.explanation: P(no rain) = 0.7 each day; independent: 0.7 x 0.7 = 0.49.
questions.theory: The complement of an event has probability 1 minus the event's probability. For independent days, multiply the daily no-rain probabilities.

questions.question: You roll two fair dice. What is the probability that the sum is 7?
questions.explanation: There are 6 favorable outcomes out of 36, so 6/36 = 1/6.
questions.theory: For two dice there are 36 equally likely outcomes. Count the ordered pairs giving the target sum and divide by 36.

questions.question: A box has 5 good and 5 defective parts. You pick two without replacement. What is the probability both are good?
questions.explanation: Without replacement: (5/10) x (4/9) = 20/90 = 2/9.
questions.theory: Without replacement the second draw's probability changes because one item is removed. Multiply the sequential conditional probabilities.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled. Let A = rolling an even number, B = rolling a number greater than 4. What is P(A or B)?
questions.theory: For overlapping events, the union's probability equals P(A)+P(B) minus the overlap. Listing outcomes confirms the count of distinct cases.

questions.question: You draw one card from a deck. What is the probability it is a face card (J, Q, or K)?
questions.explanation: There are 12 face cards out of 52, so 12/52 = 3/13.
