questions.question: A time-series line chart of unemployment is captioned 'Unemployment soaring out of control,' but the y-axis spans only 5.0% to 5.4% and the line rises from 5.1% to 5.2% over a year. What is the honest description of the change?
options: A change of about 0.1 percentage points, made to look large by the compressed axis
questions.explanation: The actual movement is roughly a tenth of a percentage point; the narrow axis range exaggerates it into a 'surge.'
questions.theory: A compressed y-axis range can turn a negligible change into a visually alarming one. Always read the axis bounds before trusting the steepness of a line.

questions.question: True or False: A dual-axis chart can be arranged so two unrelated series appear to 'move together' simply by choosing axis scales that align their peaks, even when no real relationship exists.
questions.explanation: Independent scaling of each axis lets a designer line up the curves' bumps regardless of any true association.
questions.theory: With two free axes, the designer controls each series' vertical position and amplitude. Visual 'tracking' between the lines can be an artifact of scaling rather than evidence of a relationship.

questions.question: A bubble chart sizes each bubble's DIAMETER in proportion to a country's population. Country X has twice the population of Country Y, so its bubble diameter is drawn twice as large. Why does this mislead?
options: Diameter is the correct encoding, so there is no problem
Bubbles must always be the same size
Doubling the diameter quadruples the bubble's area, overstating a 2x population as 4x
Population cannot be shown with bubbles
questions.explanation: Area scales with the square of diameter, so encoding value by diameter makes a 2x quantity appear 4x larger.
questions.theory: Bubble charts should encode value by AREA, not diameter, because viewers perceive the filled area. Mapping value to diameter inflates large values quadratically.

questions.question: A scatterplot of advertising spend vs. sales for 12 months shows a positive trend, but all 12 points come from a single store. A manager generalizes the relationship to all 4,000 stores nationwide. What is the limitation?
options: The correlation must be negative for one store
The sample is one store, so the pattern may not generalize to the broader population
Twelve points are too few to draw any line at all
questions.explanation: A relationship observed in one store may reflect that store's specifics and need not hold across thousands of different stores.
questions.theory: Generalizing from a chart requires that the data represent the target population. A pattern from a narrow, unrepresentative sample cannot be safely extended to a much larger or different group.

questions.question: A bar chart shows '90% of dentists recommend Brand X.' The bar for Brand X reaches the top and a competitor's bar is tiny, but the survey let dentists recommend MULTIPLE brands and Brand Y was recommended by 88%. What does the chart obscure?
options: The color scheme of the bars
The exact number of dentists surveyed
That nearly as many dentists also recommend the competitor
questions.explanation: Because recommendations can overlap, the near-equal 88% for the competitor is hidden by emphasizing only Brand X's 90%.
questions.theory: Selective presentation of one figure from multiple-response data can imply exclusivity that does not exist. Context about competing options is needed to interpret a single percentage.

questions.question: A line graph shows test scores rising each year, but the y-axis is reversed so that lower scores are at the top. The line slopes downward visually while scores improve. A reader skimming the shape concludes performance is declining. The core problem is that:
options: Line graphs cannot show test scores
The reversed y-axis makes improvement look like decline to anyone reading the shape rather than the labels
questions.explanation: Flipping the axis decouples the line's visual direction from the underlying improvement, fooling shape-based reading.
questions.theory: Most readers interpret a chart's overall shape before checking axis labels. A reversed axis can be technically labeled yet still produce the opposite impression of the truth.

questions.question: Two pie charts compare market share in 2010 and 2020. The 2020 pie is drawn much larger in diameter 'because the total market grew.' A slice that is 25% of both pies looks far bigger in 2020. What confusion does resizing the whole pie create?
options: Pie charts cannot be compared across years
Enlarging the entire pie conflates total market size with each slice's share, distorting share comparisons
The slices should sum to more than 100% in 2020
questions.explanation: Scaling the pie's size to show market growth makes equal percentage slices look unequal, mixing up share and absolute size.
questions.theory: Pie charts encode share, not absolute totals. Resizing the whole pie to reflect a different total breaks the reader's ability to compare proportional slices fairly between the two charts.

questions.question: True or False: A scatterplot can display the relationship between two quantitative variables, but it cannot by itself prove that changing one variable will cause the other to change.
questions.explanation: Scatterplots reveal association, but establishing causation requires controlled experiments or careful confounder adjustment, not the plot alone.
questions.theory: A scatterplot is observational evidence of how two variables co-vary. Causal claims demand additional design elements, such as randomization, that a static plot does not provide.

questions.question: A bar chart of company growth uses bars whose WIDTHS also increase along with their heights for the larger values, so big bars dominate the chart by both dimensions. Why is the width variation a problem?
options: Bars must always be exactly 1 cm wide
Width is the only correct encoding of value
Wider bars are easier to read, so it helps
Width carries no data meaning, so varying it inflates the visual weight of large bars beyond their true values
questions.explanation: Only bar height should encode value; widening large bars adds unjustified visual area that overstates them.
questions.theory: In a standard bar chart, value is encoded by length alone and bar width is constant. Letting width vary introduces an area cue that distorts the intended one-dimensional comparison.

questions.question: A line graph shows two products' sales. Product A's line is plotted on a y-axis of 0-1000 and Product B's on a SECONDARY y-axis of 0-100, with the lines crossing midway. A viewer says 'they were equal at the crossing point.' Why is that wrong?
options: Both products had zero sales there
Lines cannot cross on a chart
The crossing point reflects the two different axis scales, not equal values
Product B's axis must also be 0-1000
questions.explanation: Because each line is measured against a different axis, a visual intersection does not mean the underlying values are equal.
questions.theory: On dual-axis charts, the point where two series cross has no inherent meaning, since each is scaled independently. Equality must be read from the actual values, not the visual intersection.

questions.question: A scatterplot shows years of education vs. income for 1,000 people with a moderate positive correlation (r about 0.5). Which statement correctly describes what r tells us?
options: Education explains exactly 50% of income differences
About 25% of the variation in income is statistically associated with education via the linear fit (r-squared)
Half the people earn more with more education
questions.explanation: Since r-squared equals 0.25, the linear relationship accounts for about a quarter of the variance in income.
questions.theory: The coefficient r measures linear association strength, and r-squared gives the proportion of variance in one variable linearly explained by the other. An r of 0.5 corresponds to 25% explained variance, not 50%.

questions.question: A chart shows monthly revenue as a bar chart, with a separate 'percent change vs. last month' line overlaid on a secondary axis. In a month where revenue fell from $200k to $190k, the percent-change line dips only slightly while a casual reader sees rising bars overall. What is the safe interpretation rule?
options: Read each series against its own axis and units; bars show levels, the line shows month-over-month change
The two series must always agree in direction
Always trust the line over the bars
questions.explanation: Levels and growth rates are different quantities, so each must be read against its proper axis rather than expecting them to match.
questions.theory: A combo chart can show a level series and a rate-of-change series simultaneously. They answer different questions and need not move together, so each must be interpreted on its own axis.

questions.question: A bar chart claims 'Sales DOUBLED' and shows the 2021 bar exactly twice as tall as the 2020 bar. But the y-axis starts at $40k: 2020 was $50k and 2021 was $60k. What actually happened?
options: Sales rose 20%, but the truncated axis made the bar twice as tall
questions.explanation: With the baseline at $40k, the $50k bar is 10 units tall and the $60k bar is 20 units, so doubling the bar height hides a mere 20% real increase.
questions.theory: When a bar's baseline is not zero, its visible height is proportional to (value minus baseline), not the value. This lets a small percentage gain look like a doubling.

questions.question: True or False: A scatterplot in which the points form a perfect upward-sloping straight line always has a correlation coefficient of exactly +1, regardless of how steep the line is.
questions.explanation: Any perfectly increasing linear pattern gives r = +1, since correlation measures linearity and direction, not slope magnitude.
questions.theory: The correlation coefficient depends on how tightly points hug a straight line and the direction, not the line's steepness. A perfect positive line yields r = +1 whether shallow or steep.

questions.question: A pictograph shows coffee consumption: each cup icon equals 1,000 cups. One region has 5 full icons; another has '2.5 icons' drawn as two full cups plus a cup enlarged to look like a giant mug. Why might a reader misjudge the second region?
options: The enlarged final icon suggests more than the intended half-unit through its larger size
Coffee cannot be measured in cups
Five icons always means five thousand exactly
questions.explanation: Representing a half-unit with an oversized full cup instead of a half-cup visually overstates the partial quantity.
questions.theory: Pictographs must depict fractional units as fractions of the icon's size or area. Substituting a larger whole icon for a partial value inflates the perceived count.

questions.question: A scatterplot of 200 patients plots dosage vs. recovery time and shows essentially a random cloud with no trend (r near 0). A drug rep points to three patients in the cloud who took high doses and recovered fast, claiming 'higher doses speed recovery.' What is the flaw?
options: The overall cloud shows no association, so cherry-picking three points is not evidence of a trend
Three points are enough to establish causation
questions.explanation: Selecting a few favorable points from a trendless cloud ignores the bulk of the data, which shows no relationship.
questions.theory: A scatterplot's overall pattern, not a handful of hand-picked points, indicates whether two variables are related. Cherry-picking supportive points from a random scatter is a classic misuse.

questions.question: A grouped bar chart compares 2019 and 2023 budgets across five departments, but the 2019 bars use one y-axis scale and the 2023 bars (drawn beside them) secretly use a scale 50% larger. Side by side, 2023 always looks bigger. What is the manipulation?
options: Using two different scales for bars that are visually compared makes one year look inflated
Grouped bar charts are inherently invalid
The bars should be stacked instead
Five departments is too many to chart
questions.explanation: Bars meant to be compared directly must share one scale; a hidden larger scale for 2023 manufactures an apparent increase.
questions.theory: Direct visual comparison of bars presumes a single common axis. Applying different scales to groups that sit side by side breaks that assumption and biases the comparison.

questions.question: A line chart shows a company's cumulative total users, which can only rise or stay flat, climbing steadily. A headline reads 'User growth accelerating every month.' Why might this headline be unjustified?
options: Cumulative totals always fall over time
A rising cumulative line can occur even if monthly new users are shrinking, so it does not prove acceleration
Cumulative charts cannot be plotted as lines
The y-axis must start above zero
questions.explanation: Cumulative counts keep rising as long as any new users join, so an upward line is consistent with slowing monthly additions.
questions.theory: A cumulative (running-total) series is monotonically non-decreasing, so its upward slope reflects ongoing additions, not acceleration. Judging acceleration requires the per-period (incremental) numbers.

questions.question: A scatterplot shows height vs. shoe size for adults, with a strong positive correlation. The plot is restricted to NBA players, all between 6'6" and 7'2". The narrow height range makes the correlation look weak. What phenomenon is this?
options: Restriction of range, which can weaken an observed correlation by limiting variability
questions.explanation: Limiting the sample to a narrow band of heights reduces variation in x, which mechanically lowers the visible correlation.
questions.theory: Restriction of range occurs when a variable is sampled over a narrow interval, shrinking its variance. This typically attenuates the correlation seen in the restricted data relative to the full population.

questions.question: True or False: A bar chart that starts its y-axis at zero and uses consistent, equal intervals can still mislead if it cherry-picks which categories or time periods to include.
questions.explanation: Even a technically correct axis cannot fix selection bias in which data are shown versus omitted.
questions.theory: Axis integrity is only one dimension of honesty. A chart with a perfect axis can still mislead by selectively including or excluding categories, dates, or comparison groups.

questions.question: A heat map colors U.S. states by total number of fast-food restaurants, with darker = more. California and Texas are darkest. A reporter concludes those states have the 'worst' fast-food density. What did the map actually show?
options: Raw counts, which track population and land area rather than density
questions.explanation: Total counts are driven largely by how big and populous a state is, so the darkest states are not necessarily the densest.
questions.theory: Choropleth maps shaded by raw totals mostly reflect underlying population or area. To show density or rate, the variable must be normalized (e.g., restaurants per capita).

questions.question: A line chart of daily app downloads is extremely jagged. The analyst replaces it with a 7-day moving average, producing a smooth rising curve, and presents ONLY the smooth version as 'the real trend.' What is a fair caution?
options: Smoothing reveals the trend but hides volatility and lags real turning points, so it shouldn't replace the raw data entirely
The raw data must have been fabricated
questions.explanation: A moving average usefully clarifies trend but suppresses day-to-day variation and reacts slowly to recent changes, so showing only it omits information.
questions.theory: Smoothing techniques like moving averages trade detail for clarity of trend. They can obscure volatility and delay the appearance of reversals, so the raw series provides important context.

questions.question: A scatterplot shows a strong positive correlation between number of firefighters sent to a blaze (x) and the fire's damage in dollars (y). A columnist argues that sending fewer firefighters would reduce damage. What is the reasoning error?
options: Fire size is a confounder: bigger fires summon more firefighters AND cause more damage, so the link is non-causal
Damage cannot be measured in dollars
questions.explanation: Larger fires independently raise both the firefighter count and the damage, creating a correlation that does not imply firefighters cause damage.
questions.theory: A lurking variable, here fire severity, can drive two outcomes simultaneously and produce a misleading positive correlation. Acting on such a correlation as if it were causal leads to absurd conclusions.

questions.question: A bar chart shows test pass rates for two schools: School A 70%, School B 72%. The chart's y-axis runs 69% to 73%, so School B's bar towers over School A's. A second chart of the same data uses 0-100% and the bars look nearly identical. Which is the more honest depiction and why?
options: Neither, because bar charts cannot show percentages
The second, because the full 0-100% range shows the 2-point gap in true proportion
The first, because differences should always be magnified
questions.explanation: A zero-based percentage axis renders the small 2-point difference at its real, modest scale rather than exaggerating it.
questions.theory: For proportions, a 0-100% axis lets bar heights reflect true relative magnitude. A heavily truncated range can turn a trivial gap into a dramatic-looking one.

questions.question: A scatterplot of 50 neighborhoods shows average income (x) vs. average test score (y) with strong positive correlation. A policymaker uses it to predict an INDIVIDUAL student's score from that student's family income. What error is being risked?
options: The ecological fallacy: a relationship among group averages may not hold for individuals
Confusing the x and y axes
questions.explanation: Correlations between neighborhood averages can differ in strength or direction from the relationship among individuals within them.
questions.theory: The ecological fallacy is inferring individual-level relationships from aggregate (group-level) data. Group averages can mask or distort the patterns that hold for the individuals composing each group.

questions.question: A 'percentage stacked bar' chart shows the composition of revenue by region for each year, with every bar normalized to 100%. A viewer claims 'Asia's revenue grew from 2020 to 2021' because Asia's segment is taller in the 2021 bar. Why might this be wrong?
options: Percentages cannot exceed 100% in any bar
The years must be on the y-axis
The chart shows shares of the total, so a larger segment can occur even if Asia's absolute revenue fell while others fell more
questions.explanation: A 100%-stacked chart shows proportions, so a bigger share can result from other regions shrinking even if Asia's dollars declined.
questions.theory: Percentage-stacked charts encode composition, not absolute level. A rising share does not guarantee rising absolute value, since shares depend on every category's relative change.

questions.question: A line graph plots quarterly profit with the y-axis on a LOGARITHMIC scale. A straight rising line on this chart is interpreted by a reader as 'constant dollar growth each quarter.' What does a straight line on a log scale actually indicate?
questions.explanation: On a log y-axis, equal vertical distances are equal ratios, so a straight line means a constant growth RATE, not a constant dollar amount.
questions.theory: Logarithmic axes space values by ratio rather than by difference. A straight line therefore represents exponential (constant-percentage) growth, which is easily misread as linear if the scale is overlooked.

questions.question: A scatterplot of two variables shows a clear positive trend, but the relationship is created entirely by one extreme high-high point in the far corner; without it, the remaining points form a flat cloud. What is the correct caution?
options: The single influential point may be driving the correlation, which could vanish if it is removed
questions.explanation: A lone extreme point can dominate the correlation, so the apparent trend may be an artifact of that one observation.
questions.theory: Influential points, especially extreme ones far from the cloud, can disproportionately determine a correlation or regression line. Checking sensitivity to such points is essential before trusting the trend.

questions.question: An infographic compares two budgets with circles: Budget A ($1B) is a circle of radius 1 unit, Budget B ($4B) is drawn with radius 4 units to be 'four times bigger.' How does the visual area compare to the true 4x ratio?
options: Area is 16x, grossly overstating the 4x difference
Area is identical for both circles
questions.explanation: A circle's area scales with the square of its radius, so a 4x radius yields 16x area, far exceeding the true 4x ratio.
questions.theory: When circles encode value by radius, area grows as the radius squared, so large values are wildly exaggerated. Correct area-encoded circles scale the radius by the square root of the value ratio.

questions.question: A town finds that months with more ice cream sales also have more sunburns. What does this show?
options: The two are correlated, likely because of a shared cause like hot weather
questions.explanation: Both rise in summer heat, so they correlate without one causing the other.
questions.theory: Correlation means two things move together. It does not mean one causes the other; a third factor can drive both.

questions.question: If two things are correlated, it proves that one causes the other.
questions.explanation: Correlation alone never proves causation because other explanations may exist.
questions.theory: A correlation shows a statistical link between two variables. Establishing causation requires more, such as a controlled experiment.

questions.question: A researcher wants to learn about all adults in a country but can only study 1,000 of them. The 1,000 adults are called the:
questions.explanation: The smaller group actually studied is the sample.
questions.theory: A population is the entire group you want to understand. A sample is the subset you actually collect data from.

questions.question: In a study, the 'population' refers to:
options: The people living in big cities only
The entire group you want to draw conclusions about
questions.explanation: The population is the whole group of interest.
questions.theory: The population is everyone or everything the study aims to describe. Results from a sample are used to make claims about the population.

questions.question: A representative sample is one that:
options: Closely reflects the makeup of the whole population
questions.explanation: A representative sample mirrors the population's key characteristics.
questions.theory: A representative sample resembles the population in important ways, like age or gender mix. This lets findings generalize to the whole group.

questions.question: A magazine asks its own readers to mail in opinions about a new tax. Why might the results be biased?
options: Readers may not represent the general public
questions.explanation: Only the magazine's readers respond, so they may differ from everyone else.
questions.theory: A biased sample systematically differs from the population. Self-selected respondents often share traits that skew the results.

questions.question: A single friend's personal story about a diet is strong scientific evidence that the diet works for everyone.
questions.explanation: One story is an anecdote, not systematic data.
questions.theory: Anecdotes are individual accounts that may not generalize. Reliable conclusions come from data gathered systematically across many cases.

questions.question: A survey asks, 'Don't you agree that our wonderful mayor deserves re-election?' This question is:
options: Leading, because it pushes people toward 'yes'
questions.explanation: Praising the mayor nudges respondents toward agreement.
questions.theory: A leading question is worded to encourage a particular answer. Such wording biases the results away from people's true views.

questions.question: An opinion poll mainly measures:
options: The exact future outcome of an election
What people say they think at the time of the poll
questions.explanation: A poll captures reported opinions at a moment in time.
questions.theory: A poll or survey measures people's stated attitudes, not guaranteed facts or future events. Opinions can change after the poll is taken.

questions.question: To learn what all students at a large school think about lunch, the best approach is to:
options: Ask the first 3 students you see at the gate
Ask a random mix of students from every grade
questions.explanation: A random mix across grades best represents all students.
questions.theory: Random sampling gives every member a fair chance of selection. This reduces bias and makes the sample more representative.

questions.question: Cities with more firefighters tend to have more fire damage. The most likely explanation is:
options: Bigger cities have both more firefighters and more fires
questions.explanation: City size drives both numbers, creating a misleading link.
questions.theory: A lurking variable is a hidden factor that influences both measured variables. It can create a correlation that is not causal.

questions.question: A sample that includes only volunteers who feel strongly about a topic is likely to be unrepresentative.
questions.explanation: People with strong feelings differ from the average person.
questions.theory: Voluntary response samples attract people with intense opinions. This makes them poor representations of the broader population.

questions.question: A company says 'Most people prefer our brand' but only surveyed people leaving its own store. The problem is:
options: The sample only includes existing customers
questions.explanation: Store customers already favor the brand, biasing the result.
questions.theory: Sampling bias occurs when the chosen group is not typical of the population. Surveying only customers ignores everyone else's views.

questions.question: Which is the population in this study: 'A scientist tests a new fertilizer on 30 tomato plants to learn about all tomato plants'?
questions.explanation: All tomato plants is the group the study aims to describe.
questions.theory: The population is the full group of interest, here all tomato plants. The 30 tested plants form the sample drawn from it.

questions.question: Children with bigger feet tend to read better. The hidden reason is most likely:
options: Older children have both bigger feet and better reading skills
questions.explanation: Age explains both, so the link is not causal.
questions.theory: A confounding variable, like age, affects both measures and creates a spurious correlation. The two variables are linked only indirectly.

questions.question: A poll is conducted only by calling landline phones during work hours. Who is likely underrepresented?
options: People who work and use only cell phones
questions.explanation: Working cell-only users are hard to reach this way.
questions.theory: Coverage bias happens when some groups are systematically left out of the sample. Excluded groups can hold different views from those included.

questions.question: Increasing the sample size can fix a sample that was chosen in a biased way.
questions.explanation: A bigger biased sample is still biased.
questions.theory: Bias comes from how a sample is selected, not its size. A large but skewed sample still misrepresents the population.

questions.question: A headline reads 'People who drink coffee live longer.' Before believing coffee causes longer life, you should ask:
options: Whether coffee drinkers differ in other ways, like income or exercise
questions.explanation: Other lifestyle differences could explain the longevity.
questions.theory: Observed associations may be explained by other factors. Ruling out alternative explanations is needed before claiming causation.

questions.question: Which question is the most neutral way to ask about a policy?
options: Should we keep this excellent policy?
Do you support or oppose this policy?
Don't you think this policy is bad?
Do you support this harmful policy?
questions.explanation: Offering 'support or oppose' avoids steering the answer.
questions.theory: Neutral wording presents options evenly without loaded language. This produces more honest, less biased survey responses.

questions.question: A study finds students who eat breakfast get better grades. A reasonable cautious conclusion is:
options: Breakfast and grades are linked, but other factors may be involved
questions.explanation: The link is real but causation is not established.
questions.theory: Observational studies can show associations but not prove cause. Stable home routines, for example, might drive both habits.

questions.question: A 'census' differs from a 'sample' because a census:
options: Collects data from the entire population
Studies a small part of the group
questions.explanation: A census tries to include everyone in the population.
questions.theory: A census gathers data from every member of the population. A sample collects from only a portion and infers the rest.

questions.question: An online quiz says 'Click here if you love our show' to measure approval. This mainly captures:
options: Opinions of fans who chose to respond
A balanced view of all viewers
questions.explanation: Only motivated fans click, skewing approval upward.
questions.theory: Self-selected online polls reflect who chooses to participate. Such groups rarely represent the full audience or public.

questions.question: A poll result can change depending on how the question is worded.
questions.explanation: Wording influences how people respond.
questions.theory: Question wording, order, and tone affect answers. Carefully neutral wording is needed for trustworthy poll results.

questions.question: Sales of umbrellas and the number of car accidents both rise on the same days. The shared cause is most likely:
questions.explanation: Rain increases both umbrella use and accident risk.
questions.theory: A common cause can make two unrelated outcomes rise together. The correlation reflects the shared driver, not a direct link.

questions.question: A gym posts five glowing reviews to prove it helps everyone lose weight. The main weakness is:
options: Five stories are anecdotes, not representative data
questions.explanation: A few selected stories do not represent all members.
questions.theory: Cherry-picked anecdotes highlight successes while ignoring failures. Representative data would include everyone, not just happy cases.

questions.question: To estimate the average height of all 10-year-olds in a city, a good sample would:
options: Use only one school's basketball team
Be drawn randomly from many neighborhoods
questions.explanation: A broad random sample reflects all 10-year-olds.
questions.theory: Random selection across the population avoids over-representing any subgroup. This yields estimates that generalize to everyone.

questions.question: A survey about TV habits is given only to people at a movie theater on Friday night. This sample is biased because:
options: Friday is not a real day
Movie-goers may have unusual TV habits compared to everyone
questions.explanation: People out at the movies may differ from typical viewers.
questions.theory: Where and when a sample is taken can systematically exclude certain people. The resulting sample may not reflect the broader population.

questions.question: A representative sample tries to match the variety found in the whole population.
questions.explanation: That matching is exactly what makes a sample representative.
questions.theory: Representativeness means the sample mirrors the population's diversity. This allows conclusions to apply to the whole group.

questions.question: A study reports that towns with more churches also have more bars. The best explanation is:
options: Larger towns simply have more of everything
questions.explanation: Population size inflates counts of both buildings.
questions.theory: When two counts both scale with a third factor like population, they correlate without a causal link. This is a classic spurious correlation.

questions.question: Which statement describes what a survey measures?
options: The exact truth about the world
questions.explanation: Surveys record what respondents say, not absolute facts.
questions.theory: A survey captures self-reported responses, which may differ from reality. People can misremember, exaggerate, or answer to please.

questions.question: A weight-loss ad shows one person's amazing 'before and after.' Why is this weak evidence?
options: One person's result may not be typical and ignores those who failed
questions.explanation: A single success case is not representative.
questions.theory: A testimonial features one favorable outcome and omits the rest. Without data on all users, it cannot show typical results.

questions.question: A researcher surveys 500 people to understand 5 million voters. The 5 million voters are the:
questions.explanation: The full group of interest is the population.
questions.theory: The population is the entire set the study targets. The 500 surveyed form the sample used to estimate the population's views.

questions.question: A poll asks only people standing outside a sports stadium whether the city should fund a new stadium. The likely bias is:
options: The poll has too few questions
Sports fans probably favor the stadium more than average
questions.explanation: Fans at a stadium are predisposed to support it.
questions.theory: Sampling people connected to the topic skews results toward that interest. A representative sample would include the wider community.

questions.question: Correlation between two variables can sometimes happen purely by coincidence.
questions.explanation: Some correlations arise by chance with no real connection.
questions.theory: With enough variables, some will correlate by chance alone. Such coincidental correlations have no meaningful underlying cause.

questions.question: A company surveys employees and asks them to write their names on the form before rating their boss. This may bias answers because:
options: Names take too long to write
Employees may fear giving honest negative ratings
questions.explanation: Lack of anonymity discourages honest criticism.
questions.theory: When responses are not anonymous, people may answer to avoid consequences. This response bias distorts the true picture.

questions.question: Which is the best example of data rather than an anecdote?
options: A survey of 10,000 people tracking smoking and health outcomes
My uncle smoked and lived to 90
A story a neighbor told me
questions.explanation: A large systematic survey is data, not a single story.
questions.theory: Data come from systematic collection across many cases. Anecdotes are isolated stories that may not reflect general patterns.

questions.question: A news report says a study 'found a link' between two things. 'A link' most accurately means:
options: The two things were found to be associated
One thing definitely causes the other
questions.explanation: A 'link' signals association, not proven cause.
questions.theory: Phrases like 'linked to' describe correlation. They do not establish that one variable causes changes in the other.

questions.question: To fairly sample opinions of a whole country, a poll should:
options: Include people from many regions, ages, and backgrounds
questions.explanation: Broad inclusion makes the sample represent the country.
questions.theory: A representative national sample spans the population's regions and demographics. Narrow sampling misses important segments.

questions.question: If a survey only reaches people who already agree with its sponsor, the results are trustworthy for the whole public.
questions.explanation: Such a sample is biased and cannot represent everyone.
questions.theory: A sample skewed toward one viewpoint cannot speak for the general public. Selection bias undermines the survey's validity.

questions.question: A study finds that people who use sunscreen get more skin cancer. The likely confounder is:
options: People with lots of sun exposure both use more sunscreen and face more risk
Sunscreen is harmless so the study is fake
questions.explanation: Sun exposure drives both sunscreen use and risk.
questions.theory: A confounder is linked to both variables and explains their association. Here, time in the sun confounds the sunscreen-cancer link.

questions.question: A teacher claims 'Students who sit in front get better grades, so sitting in front raises grades.' A flaw is:
options: Motivated students may choose front seats already
questions.explanation: Seat choice may reflect existing motivation, not cause grades.
questions.theory: When people choose their own group, pre-existing differences can explain outcomes. This makes causal claims unreliable.

questions.question: A 'biased sample' is one that:
options: Systematically over- or under-represents parts of the population
questions.explanation: Bias means some groups are over- or under-counted.
questions.theory: A biased sample fails to reflect the population because of how it was selected. Certain groups are favored or excluded systematically.

questions.question: A vitamin maker shares 3 customer thank-you emails as proof its pills cure colds. The strongest objection is:
options: Three happy customers are not representative evidence
questions.explanation: A handful of testimonials does not prove general effectiveness.
questions.theory: Selected testimonials ignore everyone the product did not help. Proper evidence requires data on all users, including failures.

questions.question: Which of these is a population, given the goal 'understand spending of all U.S. teenagers'?
questions.explanation: All U.S. teenagers is the target group.
questions.theory: The population is the complete group the study describes. The mall sample is just the portion measured to estimate it.

questions.question: Asking a fair, neutral question is important for getting trustworthy survey results.
questions.explanation: Neutral questions reduce bias in responses.
questions.theory: Fair wording lets respondents answer truthfully without being nudged. This improves the accuracy and credibility of survey data.

questions.question: A study shows neighborhoods with more streetlights have more reported crime. A cautious reader thinks:
options: Busier or denser areas may have both more lights and more reports
questions.explanation: Density or activity could drive both, not a causal link.
questions.theory: Correlation can stem from a shared driver like population density. Jumping to causation ignores these alternative explanations.

questions.question: An internet poll on a fishing website asks 'Is fishing the best hobby?' The result will likely be biased because:
options: The site attracts people who already love fishing
questions.explanation: Visitors already favor fishing, skewing the poll.
questions.theory: Polls placed where a specific group gathers attract that group's views. The sample is not representative of the general public.

questions.question: Why do good studies use a sample instead of always studying the whole population?
options: Samples are always more accurate than a full census
Studying everyone is often too costly or impractical
questions.explanation: A sample saves time and money when a full count is impractical.
questions.theory: Measuring an entire population is often expensive or impossible. A well-chosen sample gives reliable estimates more efficiently.

questions.question: A friend says 'Seatbelts are pointless; my cousin survived a crash without one.' This reasoning is weak because:
options: One survival story does not outweigh broad safety data
questions.explanation: A lone anecdote cannot overturn large-scale evidence.
questions.theory: Single anecdotes can contradict overall trends by chance. Decisions should rest on systematic data, not isolated stories.

questions.question: A poll reaches only people who answer their door on a weekday morning. Who is most likely missed?
options: People working a daytime job away from home
People who are home all day
questions.explanation: Daytime workers are away and get excluded.
questions.theory: The timing of contact can systematically exclude certain groups. Their absence biases the sample away from the full population.

questions.question: A radio station asks listeners to call in and vote on whether a new highway should be built. 80% of callers say yes. Why might this result be misleading?
options: 80% is too small a percentage to mean anything
Phone votes are always more accurate than online votes
The sample relies on voluntary response, so people with strong opinions are overrepresented
Radio listeners are a perfect cross-section of the population
questions.explanation: Voluntary-response samples overrepresent those who feel strongly enough to act, biasing the result.
questions.theory: Voluntary-response polls let respondents select themselves into the sample. People with intense opinions are far more likely to participate, so the results rarely reflect the broader population.

questions.question: Researchers find that towns with more firefighters tend to have more fire damage. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The correlation proves firefighters are ineffective
Reducing the number of firefighters would lower fire damage
A confounding variable, town size, drives both numbers up
questions.explanation: Larger towns have both more firefighters and more fires, so town size confounds the relationship.
questions.theory: A confounding variable influences both the supposed cause and the supposed effect, creating a misleading association. Controlling for it often makes the apparent relationship disappear.

questions.question: A gym surveys only members leaving the building at 6 a.m. about how often people exercise. This is an example of what kind of bias?
questions.explanation: Sampling whoever is easiest to reach (early-morning gym-goers) is convenience sampling and skews toward frequent exercisers.
questions.theory: Convenience sampling draws from whoever is easiest to access rather than a representative group. The resulting sample often differs systematically from the target population.

questions.question: A study reports the 'average' household income in a neighborhood is $250,000, but most families earn around $60,000. What likely happened?
options: Everyone in the neighborhood earns exactly $250,000
A few extremely high incomes pulled the mean upward
The sample was too large to be reliable
The median was reported instead of the mean
questions.explanation: A small number of very large incomes inflate the mean, making it unrepresentative of the typical household.
questions.theory: The mean is sensitive to outliers, while the median is not. When a distribution is skewed, the mean can sit far from where most data points cluster.

questions.question: A poll of 1,000 people reports 52% support with a margin of error of plus or minus 3 points. This means the true support is almost certainly between 49% and 55%.
questions.explanation: The margin of error defines a confidence interval, so the true value most plausibly lies within 49% to 55%.
questions.theory: Margin of error expresses the range within which the true population value is likely to fall, given sampling variability. A 3-point margin around 52% yields an interval of roughly 49% to 55%.

questions.question: In a drug trial, one group receives the new medication and another receives a sugar pill. The sugar-pill group is best described as the:
questions.explanation: The group receiving no active treatment serves as the control for comparison.
questions.theory: A control group does not receive the experimental treatment and provides a baseline. Comparing treatment and control isolates the treatment's effect from other factors.

questions.question: Ice cream sales and drowning deaths both rise in summer. A reporter claims ice cream causes drowning. What is the flaw?
options: Correlation here is negative, not positive
The sample size is too small
Hot weather is a confounder driving both variables
Drowning actually causes ice cream sales
questions.explanation: Warm weather increases both ice cream consumption and swimming, so it confounds the relationship.
questions.theory: Two variables can move together because a third factor influences both. Without ruling out such confounders, you cannot infer that one variable causes the other.

questions.question: An online retailer emails a satisfaction survey only to customers who left a product review. Why is the sample biased?
options: The survey is too long to complete
All customers were equally likely to respond
Reviewers are a self-selected group unlike typical customers
questions.explanation: Customers who write reviews self-select and tend to hold stronger opinions than the average buyer.
questions.theory: Self-selection bias arises when individuals choose whether to be in a sample. Those who opt in often differ from the general population in ways that distort results.

questions.question: A headline reads 'People who drink red wine live longer.' Before accepting causation, what should you most consider?
options: Whether the study was published recently
Whether wine drinkers differ in diet, income, or exercise
Whether red wine tastes better than white
Whether the headline used a large font
questions.explanation: Lifestyle differences between wine drinkers and others could explain the longevity gap rather than the wine itself.
questions.theory: Observational studies cannot easily separate a treatment from correlated lifestyle factors. Such factors must be considered before attributing an outcome to a single cause.

questions.question: Increasing a poll's sample size from 500 to 2,000 people generally narrows the margin of error.
questions.explanation: Larger samples reduce sampling variability, shrinking the margin of error.
questions.theory: Margin of error decreases as sample size grows, roughly in proportion to one over the square root of the sample size. Bigger samples give more precise estimates.

questions.question: A magazine prints a survey form for readers to mail back about their reading habits. The results are most affected by:
questions.explanation: Only motivated readers mail back forms, making this a voluntary-response sample.
questions.theory: Mail-in survey forms produce voluntary-response samples. Respondents are typically more engaged or opinionated than non-respondents, biasing conclusions.

questions.question: A study shows students who use tutoring services score higher on exams. Why might tutoring not be the sole cause?
options: Students who seek tutoring may already be more motivated
Tutoring lowers test scores in reality
questions.explanation: Motivation is a confounder: motivated students both seek tutoring and study harder, inflating the apparent effect.
questions.theory: When participants choose a treatment, pre-existing differences can drive outcomes. Self-selected motivation often confounds observational education studies.

questions.question: To compare a new fertilizer, a farmer applies it to one field and nothing to an adjacent field. The untreated field functions as the:
questions.explanation: The untreated field is the control, providing a baseline for comparison.
questions.theory: A control group experiences the same conditions except for the treatment. It reveals what would have happened without the intervention.

questions.question: A company claims its workers earn an 'average' salary of $90,000. The CEO earns $5 million; most workers earn $40,000. Which statistic would better represent a typical worker?
questions.explanation: The median is resistant to the CEO's extreme salary and better reflects the typical worker.
questions.theory: In skewed distributions, the median better describes a typical value than the mean. Extreme high earners can inflate the mean far above what most people make.

questions.question: Interviewers survey shoppers at a luxury mall to estimate average national spending on clothes. The main problem is:
options: The sample conveniently chosen is not representative of the nation
The estimate will be too low
questions.explanation: Luxury-mall shoppers are a convenience sample that spends more than the national average, biasing the estimate upward.
questions.theory: Convenience samples taken from an unrepresentative location systematically misestimate population parameters. Generalizing from them to a broader population is unreliable.

questions.question: A website poll shows 90% oppose a tax, but only people who clicked an angry headline could vote. What weakens this poll most?
options: Self-selection by readers already opposed to the tax
The tax rate was stated incorrectly
The poll had too many respondents
questions.explanation: Readers drawn to an angry headline self-select into opposition, distorting the result.
questions.theory: When the recruitment channel attracts a particular viewpoint, self-selection bias dominates. The sample reflects who chose to participate, not the population.

questions.question: A study finds children with bigger feet read better. The most plausible confounder is:
options: Age, since older children have bigger feet and read better
questions.explanation: Age drives both foot size and reading ability, explaining the correlation without any direct link.
questions.theory: A lurking variable like age can produce a strong correlation between two otherwise unrelated traits. Identifying it dissolves the false impression of causation.

questions.question: If two variables are strongly correlated, it follows that one of them must cause the other.
questions.explanation: Correlation can arise from confounders, coincidence, or reverse causation, so it does not establish causation.
questions.theory: Correlation measures association, not mechanism. A relationship may be explained by a third variable, chance, or reversed direction of effect.

questions.question: A clinic studies a diet by comparing volunteers who chose the diet against those who did not. Why is this weaker than a randomized experiment?
options: The two groups are guaranteed identical
Self-selected dieters may differ systematically from non-dieters
Randomization is illegal in nutrition studies
questions.explanation: Because participants chose their group, pre-existing differences confound the comparison.
questions.theory: Randomized assignment balances confounding variables across groups on average. Without it, self-selected groups can differ in ways that bias the estimated effect.

questions.question: A poll reports 47% support plus or minus 4 points, and 44% oppose plus or minus 4 points. What is the safest conclusion?
options: Opposition is winning by a wide margin
The race is effectively too close to call
questions.explanation: The overlapping margins of error mean the difference is not statistically distinguishable.
questions.theory: When two estimates' margins of error overlap substantially, the apparent gap may be due to sampling noise. Such differences should be treated as inconclusive.

questions.question: A teacher tests a new method on her honors class and compares scores to last year's regular class. The flaw is that:
options: The groups differ in ability, confounding the comparison
The new method has no effect by definition
Scores from last year do not exist
questions.explanation: Comparing honors students to regular students confounds the method's effect with student ability.
questions.theory: Valid experiments compare groups that are similar except for the treatment. Pre-existing ability differences act as a confounder and undermine causal claims.

questions.question: A survey of pet owners is conducted by handing forms only to people at a veterinary clinic on vaccination day. This sampling method is:
options: A census of all pet owners
A convenience sample skewed toward responsible owners
questions.explanation: Owners who vaccinate are conveniently available but more conscientious than typical owners.
questions.theory: Convenience sampling at a specific venue captures a subgroup with shared traits. The sample may not represent the full population of interest.

questions.question: Sales of sunglasses correlate with sunscreen sales. Claiming sunglasses cause sunscreen purchases ignores that:
options: Sunny weather drives demand for both products
questions.explanation: Sunny weather is a common cause boosting both products, so neither causes the other.
questions.theory: A shared driver can produce correlation between two effects. Mistaking this for direct causation between the effects is a classic error.

questions.question: A fitness app reports its users 'lose an average of 15 pounds,' but only users who reached their goal are counted. This is misleading because of:
options: The use of pounds instead of kilograms
questions.explanation: Counting only successful users excludes those who failed, inflating the apparent average loss.
questions.theory: Survivorship bias occurs when only 'surviving' cases are analyzed and failures are dropped. The remaining sample overstates success because dropouts are invisible.

questions.question: A larger sample size can fix bias caused by sampling only one unrepresentative group.
questions.explanation: Increasing size of a biased sample produces a more precise but still biased estimate.
questions.theory: Sample size affects precision, not bias. If the sampling method systematically excludes parts of the population, collecting more data does not correct the distortion.

questions.question: Researchers observe that neighborhoods with more parks have lower obesity rates and conclude parks reduce obesity. A confounder could be:
options: Household income affecting both park access and health
The number of trees per park
questions.explanation: Wealthier areas have both more parks and lower obesity, so income confounds the link.
questions.theory: Socioeconomic status frequently confounds observational health studies. It can independently cause both the exposure and the outcome being studied.

questions.question: In an experiment, neither participants nor researchers know who receives the real treatment. The control group exists primarily to:
options: Provide a comparison baseline for the treatment effect
questions.explanation: The control group gives a baseline against which the treatment's effect is measured.
questions.theory: A control group reveals the outcome absent the treatment, enabling a fair comparison. Differences between groups can then be attributed to the treatment.

questions.question: A news story says the 'average' wait time at a hospital is 20 minutes, but half of patients wait over an hour. This suggests the average reported is:
options: Likely a mean pulled down by many quick visits, hiding long waits
The median, dragged down by very short waits
questions.explanation: A skewed mix of very short and very long waits can yield a low mean that masks frequent long waits.
questions.theory: A single average can conceal wide variation in a skewed distribution. Reporting only a measure of center hides the spread that matters to individuals.

questions.question: A talk show invites viewers to text 'YES' or 'NO' on a controversial issue. The biggest threat to the poll's validity is:
options: Self-selected viewers with strong feelings dominate
Each viewer can only vote once
Texting is too slow to record votes
questions.explanation: Only viewers who feel strongly bother to text, producing a voluntary-response bias.
questions.theory: Voluntary-response polls capture the most motivated respondents. Their views can diverge sharply from the silent majority who do not participate.

questions.question: A study finds people who take vitamins are healthier. Before crediting the vitamins, you should ask whether:
options: Vitamin takers also tend to eat well and exercise
questions.explanation: Health-conscious behaviors that accompany vitamin use could explain the health difference instead of the vitamins.
questions.theory: People who adopt one healthy habit often adopt others, creating correlated confounders. These must be ruled out before attributing benefits to a single behavior.

questions.question: A polling firm reaches respondents only by calling landline phones during weekday afternoons. This likely undersamples:
options: Working adults and younger people without landlines
questions.explanation: Weekday-afternoon landline calls miss employed and younger respondents, biasing the sample.
questions.theory: The sampling frame and timing determine who can be reached. Systematic exclusion of groups produces coverage bias that distorts population estimates.

questions.question: A randomized controlled experiment is generally better than an observational study at supporting cause-and-effect claims.
questions.explanation: Randomization balances confounders across groups, allowing stronger causal inference than observation alone.
questions.theory: Randomized assignment distributes lurking variables evenly between treatment and control on average. This control over confounding is what licenses causal conclusions.

questions.question: A report states that counties with more doctors have higher cancer rates and implies doctors cause cancer. A better explanation is:
options: Doctors detect more cancer, and populations differ in age and size
The data are about heart disease
questions.explanation: Greater detection and differences in population age and size explain the pattern, not causation by doctors.
questions.theory: Detection bias and demographic confounders can create spurious associations. Higher measured rates may reflect more diagnosis rather than more disease caused.

questions.question: A school newspaper asks students to vote online for the 'best teacher,' but only tech-club members see the link. The result is biased by:
options: Limited, self-selected access to the poll
questions.explanation: Restricting the poll to tech-club members yields a self-selected, unrepresentative sample.
questions.theory: When access to a survey is limited to a subgroup, the sample cannot represent the whole population. Results reflect that subgroup's preferences only.

questions.question: A company says '9 out of 10 dentists recommend our toothpaste,' but dentists could pick several brands. This claim is misleading because:
options: Recommending it does not mean preferring it over competitors
Ten dentists is a huge sample
questions.explanation: Dentists may recommend many brands, so the statistic does not show the product is preferred.
questions.theory: Ambiguous wording can make a weak finding sound strong. A claim that respondents 'recommend' something says nothing about relative preference if multiple choices are allowed.

questions.question: To test whether music improves productivity, a manager lets employees choose whether to wear headphones. The design is flawed because:
options: Music has no effect on anyone
Employees who choose music may already work differently
questions.explanation: Allowing employees to self-select into the music group confounds the comparison with their existing work styles.
questions.theory: Self-selected treatment groups differ from controls in unmeasured ways. Random assignment is needed to attribute differences to the treatment itself.

questions.question: A poll of 100 voters reports 60% support with a margin of error of plus or minus 10 points. Compared to a poll of 1,000 voters, this margin is:
options: Smaller because fewer people are easier to count
Larger because the smaller sample is less precise
Exactly the same regardless of size
questions.explanation: A smaller sample produces greater sampling variability and thus a wider margin of error.
questions.theory: Margin of error grows as sample size shrinks. Smaller samples give noisier, less precise estimates of the population value.

questions.question: A study reports that cities with more bicycles have cleaner air and concludes bicycles clean the air. The leap ignores that:
options: Cities promoting cycling may also limit cars and industry
questions.explanation: Policies that encourage cycling often also reduce cars and industry, which is the real driver of cleaner air.
questions.theory: An association can be produced by broader factors correlated with the measured variable. Policy or context can confound a simple cause-and-effect story.

questions.question: A voluntary online poll on a news site reliably represents the views of all the site's readers.
questions.explanation: Only readers who choose to vote participate, so the poll suffers from voluntary-response bias.
questions.theory: Voluntary online polls reflect self-selected respondents, not the full readership. Those who vote tend to feel more strongly than those who abstain.

questions.question: In a soap experiment, both groups wash with identical routines, but only one group's soap contains a new additive. The matched routine ensures that:
options: The additive is the main difference between the groups
The groups must get different results
questions.explanation: Keeping everything identical except the additive isolates its effect by holding other factors constant.
questions.theory: Good experimental design holds all conditions equal except the treatment. This isolation lets observed differences be attributed to the treatment.

questions.question: A real-estate ad boasts the 'average' home price on a street is $400,000, citing one $2 million mansion among nine $200,000 homes. The average is misleading because:
options: The median equals the mean here
The mansion outlier inflates the mean above typical homes
Nine homes is too many to average
questions.explanation: The single expensive mansion pulls the mean far above the price of a typical home on the street.
questions.theory: Outliers exert strong leverage on the mean. In skewed data, citing the mean can paint a misleading picture of what is typical.

questions.question: A survey concludes 'most Americans support a policy,' but it only sampled visitors to one political website. The conclusion overreaches because:
options: Surveys must include millions of people
The sample is not representative of all Americans
questions.explanation: Visitors to a single political website are not representative of the national population.
questions.theory: Generalizing from a narrow, self-selected sample to a broad population is invalid. The sampling frame must match the population the claim is about.

questions.question: Researchers note that students who eat breakfast get better grades and recommend mandatory breakfast. The recommendation may fail because:
options: Breakfast cannot be served at school
Stable home environments may cause both breakfast and good grades
questions.explanation: A supportive home life could produce both regular breakfasts and better grades, confounding the link.
questions.theory: An observed correlation may stem from a background factor affecting both variables. Intervening on one variable may not change the outcome if the real cause lies elsewhere.

questions.question: A weight-loss ad shows results 'based on customers who completed the full program.' This selective reporting is a form of:
questions.explanation: Reporting only finishers ignores those who quit, overstating the program's success.
questions.theory: Survivorship bias inflates apparent success by analyzing only those who remained. Excluded dropouts often represent failures that would lower the reported effect.

questions.question: A study claims people who sleep with the light on are more likely to be nearsighted, implying light causes it. A later finding suggested parents' nearsightedness explained both. This is an example of:
options: A confounding variable explaining the association
questions.explanation: Parental nearsightedness influenced both the lighting choice and the children's vision, confounding the original claim.
questions.theory: A confounder linked to both variables can masquerade as a causal relationship. Discovering it reveals the original cause-and-effect claim as spurious.

questions.question: A poll's margin of error is reported as plus or minus 3 points, but the question was worded to favor one answer. The margin of error does NOT account for:
questions.explanation: Margin of error reflects only random sampling error, not bias from leading questions.
questions.theory: Margin of error quantifies random sampling variability alone. Systematic errors like biased wording are not captured and can distort results beyond the stated margin.

questions.question: A gym posts that members who hired trainers 'saw 30% better results.' Since members chose whether to hire trainers, the comparison is weakened by:
options: Self-selection of motivated members into training
questions.explanation: Members who hire trainers tend to be more committed, so self-selection inflates the trainers' apparent effect.
questions.theory: Self-selection means the groups differ before any treatment begins. Without randomization, those differences confound the measured effect.

questions.question: A charity reports the 'average donation' was $500, but most people gave $25 and one donor gave $1 million. To describe the typical gift, you should report the:
questions.explanation: The median ignores the single huge gift and better reflects what most donors gave.
questions.theory: When one extreme value dominates, the mean misrepresents the typical case. The median provides a more robust summary of central tendency in skewed data.

questions.question: To evaluate a tutoring program fairly, students are randomly assigned to receive tutoring or not. Randomization mainly helps by:
options: Balancing confounding variables across the two groups
questions.explanation: Random assignment spreads confounders evenly, so differences in outcomes can be attributed to the tutoring.
questions.theory: Randomization tends to equalize known and unknown confounders between groups. This balance is what allows a causal interpretation of the treatment effect.

questions.question: An airline brags that '95% of surveyed passengers were satisfied,' but the survey was handed out only to first-class travelers. The result is biased because:
options: First-class passengers represent all passengers equally
The convenience sample of premium flyers is not representative
questions.explanation: Surveying only first-class passengers conveniently samples a group likely more satisfied than economy travelers.
questions.theory: Sampling only a privileged subgroup yields a convenience sample that overstates favorable outcomes. Such results cannot be generalized to all customers.

questions.question: A university finds that within every department, women are admitted at a HIGHER rate than men, yet the overall university-wide admission rate is higher for men. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Women disproportionately applied to departments with low overall admission rates
Men were given secret preference at the aggregate level only
A data-entry error, since the aggregate must match the within-group pattern
The sample of women was too small to be reliable
questions.explanation: This is Simpson's paradox: a confounding variable (which department applicants chose) reverses the direction of association when groups are pooled.
questions.theory: Simpson's paradox occurs when an association that holds within every subgroup reverses or vanishes after the subgroups are combined. It arises because a lurking variable is unevenly distributed across the groups being aggregated.

questions.question: During WWII, analysts examined returning bombers and found bullet holes concentrated on the wings and fuselage, rarely on the engines. Abraham Wald recommended adding armor to the ENGINES. What reasoning justifies his counterintuitive advice?
options: Armor is cheaper to install near the engines
Bullet holes in the wings indicated the wings were the weakest structurally
Engines are heavier and need extra reinforcement regardless of damage
Planes hit in the engines were the ones that did not return, so that area was underrepresented in the sample
questions.explanation: The data came only from survivors; missing engine damage signaled that engine hits were fatal, a classic case of survivorship bias.
questions.theory: Survivorship bias arises when an analysis includes only the cases that 'survived' some selection process, omitting those that did not. The absence of a feature among survivors can indicate it was lethal, not unimportant.

questions.question: A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. A test is 99% accurate (both sensitivity and specificity). A person tests positive. Roughly what is the probability they actually have the disease?
questions.explanation: With 1 true positive versus about 10 false positives per 1,000 people, the positive predictive value is roughly 1/11 ≈ 9%.
questions.theory: Base-rate neglect is the tendency to ignore the prior probability (prevalence) when interpreting a test result. When a condition is rare, even a highly accurate test produces many false positives relative to true positives.

questions.question: A researcher runs 20 independent hypothesis tests, each at α = 0.05, on data where no real effects exist. What is the approximate probability of getting at least one 'significant' result by chance?
questions.explanation: P(at least one false positive) = 1 − 0.95^20 ≈ 0.64, illustrating the multiple-comparisons problem.
questions.theory: When many tests are run, the family-wise probability of at least one false positive grows quickly even if each test uses α = 0.05. Corrections such as Bonferroni adjust the threshold to control this inflation.

questions.question: Because a well-conducted randomized controlled trial randomly assigns treatment, it can support causal claims even about confounders the researchers never measured.
questions.explanation: Randomization balances both measured and unmeasured confounders in expectation, which is why RCTs license causal inference.
questions.theory: Randomized experiments distribute confounding variables evenly across groups on average, including ones nobody thought to record. Observational studies can only adjust for confounders that were measured.

questions.question: Students who scored worst on a first exam were given tutoring, and on the retest their average score rose substantially. A skeptic argues the tutoring effect may be overstated. Which phenomenon supports the skeptic even if tutoring did nothing?
questions.explanation: Extreme low scorers tend to score closer to the mean on retest regardless of intervention, inflating apparent gains.
questions.theory: Regression to the mean is the tendency for extreme measurements to be followed by ones closer to the average, due to the random component of any measurement. Selecting a group because it is extreme guarantees apparent movement toward the mean on remeasurement.

questions.question: A study finds that countries with higher average chocolate consumption have more Nobel laureates per capita. A writer concludes that eating chocolate boosts an individual's chance of winning a Nobel Prize. What is the primary flaw?
questions.explanation: Inferring about individuals from country-level (aggregate) correlations is the ecological fallacy.
questions.theory: The ecological fallacy occurs when conclusions about individuals are drawn from data measured at the group level. Group-level correlations need not hold at the individual level and may be driven by confounders like national wealth.

questions.question: An online poll on a fitness website asks 'Do you exercise daily?' and reports that 78% of respondents do. A journalist cites this as evidence most adults exercise daily. The core problem is that the sample is:
options: Affected by regression to the mean
questions.explanation: Visitors to a fitness site who choose to respond are not representative of all adults, biasing the estimate upward.
questions.theory: Selection bias arises when the sample is collected in a way that systematically differs from the target population. Self-selected and convenience samples often over- or under-represent traits correlated with the survey's topic.

questions.question: A nutrition team measures 50 dietary factors against one health outcome, finds that 'kale intake' is significant at p = 0.04, and publishes only that finding. Why should readers be cautious?
options: Kale cannot affect health, so the result must be fraud
A p-value of 0.04 is mathematically impossible with 50 variables
Testing 50 factors and reporting the one that crossed 0.05 is p-hacking via selective reporting
Larger samples always reduce p-values to zero
questions.explanation: Screening many variables and reporting only the significant one inflates the false-positive rate without correction.
questions.theory: P-hacking refers to analytic choices (testing many variables, subgroups, or model specifications) that increase the chance of a significant result. Reporting only the 'winners' without accounting for the number tested makes findings unreliable.

questions.question: An observational study reports that people who drink red wine have lower heart-disease rates. Which conclusion is BEST supported?
options: Everyone should drink red wine to prevent heart disease
Red wine has no relationship with heart disease
Drinking red wine causes lower heart-disease risk
Red wine is associated with lower heart-disease risk, but confounding (e.g., income, diet) cannot be ruled out
questions.explanation: Observational data can establish association but cannot rule out confounders, so only a correlational claim is warranted.
questions.theory: Observational studies measure variables without manipulating them, so association cannot be cleanly separated from confounding. Causal language requires either a randomized experiment or strong causal-inference assumptions.

questions.question: A hospital reports that overall mortality is higher among patients treated with a new surgical technique than with the old one. Yet for both low-risk and high-risk patients separately, the new technique has lower mortality. What likely produced the reversal?
options: Regression to the mean inflated old-technique survival
The new technique was disproportionately used on high-risk patients
The two subgroups are too similar to compare
questions.explanation: If the better technique was applied mostly to sicker patients, aggregation can reverse the within-group advantage (Simpson's paradox).
questions.theory: Simpson's paradox in medicine often arises from confounding by indication: the more effective or aggressive treatment is given to patients with worse baseline prognosis. Stratifying by risk reveals the true direction of the effect.

questions.question: A mutual-fund advertisement lists the 10-year returns of all funds the company currently offers; funds that performed poorly and were closed are not shown. The advertised average return is therefore likely biased upward.
questions.explanation: Excluding closed (failed) funds is survivorship bias, which inflates the reported average performance.
questions.theory: Survivorship bias in finance occurs when only currently existing funds are analyzed, omitting those that closed due to poor returns. This systematically overstates historical performance of the surviving set.

questions.question: A security analyst claims a profiling algorithm is excellent because it flags 95% of actual threats. To assess whether acting on a flag is sensible, what additional quantity is most essential?
options: The base rate of threats and the false-positive rate
The cost of the algorithm's software license
The total number of people screened last year
questions.explanation: Without the base rate and false-positive rate, high sensitivity alone says nothing about how many flags are real.
questions.theory: Base-rate neglect causes people to overvalue sensitivity (true-positive rate) while ignoring how rare the condition is. The positive predictive value depends jointly on prevalence, sensitivity, and specificity.

questions.question: A team explores a dataset, notices an interesting pattern, then runs a hypothesis test on the SAME data to confirm it and reports p = 0.01. Why is this p-value misleading?
options: The hypothesis was generated from the data it was tested on, so the test is not truly confirmatory
0.01 is too small to be trustworthy in any context
The sample was randomized, which invalidates p-values
questions.explanation: Testing a hypothesis on the same data that suggested it (double-dipping) invalidates the p-value's interpretation.
questions.theory: Generating and testing a hypothesis on the same dataset, sometimes called HARKing or double-dipping, capitalizes on noise patterns. Confirmatory inference requires the hypothesis to be specified before seeing the test data, ideally on a held-out sample.

questions.question: A startup advisor says, 'Most successful founders dropped out of college, so dropping out improves your odds of success.' Which bias most directly undermines this inference?
questions.explanation: Looking only at successful founders ignores the many dropouts who failed, so the success rate of dropping out is unknown.
questions.theory: Survivorship bias arises when only successes are examined, hiding the denominator of failures. Without the base of all dropouts, the conditional probability of success given dropping out cannot be estimated.

questions.question: Researchers report that a sports drink improved performance (p = 0.048) in a trial. A meta-analyst worries that other labs ran similar trials with null results that were never published. This concern is about:
questions.explanation: Unpublished null results bias the literature toward false or inflated positive effects, the file-drawer problem.
questions.theory: Publication bias occurs when studies with significant or positive results are more likely to be published than null ones. The resulting 'file drawer' of unpublished nulls makes the published literature overstate true effects.

questions.question: A school district notes that schools with the highest test scores in one year tend to show smaller gains the next year, while the lowest-scoring schools show larger gains. An administrator credits a new policy targeting low schools. What confound must be addressed first?
options: Regression to the mean across years
questions.explanation: Extreme-scoring schools naturally drift toward the mean the next year, mimicking policy effects.
questions.theory: When schools (or any units) are ranked by a noisy measure, the extremes contain transient luck that does not persist. Year-over-year movement toward the average can masquerade as the effect of an intervention.

questions.question: Statistical significance at p < 0.05 in a study with 100,000 participants guarantees that the effect is large enough to matter in practice.
questions.explanation: Very large samples can make trivially small effects statistically significant, so significance does not imply practical importance.
